review_text:
Makoto Shinkai's The Garden of Words is one of those films that earns the word "immersive" without having to ask for it. From the first scene, it pulls you in — not with action, not with urgency, but with the quiet insistence of rain on glass. You settle into it before you've even decided to. The visuals hit first, and they hit hard. Every backdrop — wet garden tiles, light fracturing through leaves, the particular grey of a Tokyo sky about to open up — looks less like animation and more like memory. Character designs are precise without being sterile. There's a tactile quality to everyscene, a feeling that if you reached into the screen you'd come back with damp fingers. It's the kind of craft that makes you pause mid-scene just to sit with what you're looking at. Knowing this came out in 2013 almost feels like a trick.

The story doesn't try to compete with the visuals on spectacle. It doesn't need to. A teenage boy and an older woman keep finding each other in the same park gazebo every rainy morning — him skipping school, her reasons murkier and more loaded. What builds between them isn't loud. It's accumulated. Silences that stretch a beat too long. Small rituals that start to mean more than they should. The film understands that connection, real connection, rarely announces itself.

"Even if it doesn't rain, I'll stay with you."

That line — rooted in an ancient Japanese tanka poem — does the heavy lifting for the film's central theme without ever forcing it. Rain here is structure. It's permission. It's the condition under which these two people are allowed to exist in the same space, sheltered from whatever waits for them outside. The promise to stay even without it is, quietly, everything.

What stays with you after the credits roll isn't plot — the plot, honestly, is not the point. It's the film's argument about what genuine human connection actually does to a person. Not the romanticized version where someone saves you and you become whole. The subtler, m

Y no podia faltar Radwimps para el cierre nunca decepciona

My honest opinion on K-ON!: I absolutely LOVE the show! Yui Hirasawa is my spirit animal >v< It was better than other anime in my favorites list, and also my favorite (alongside Futari wa Precure which is my fav magical girl anime). Also I wish everyone will love this show for generations, as their music is peak. Also I pray to the anime gods that a Sarah & Duck anime will be made by Kyoto Animation, so that would be a true banger to their lineup (as it would be adapted from the 2013 British cartoon, so KyoAni, take some notes and then give the BBC their permission first, orjust ask Tim O'Sullivan).

The story was a well-followed adaptation of the original, as it was because of their chemistry, seen in the manga.
Yukiko Horiguchi's designs are way peaker than other artists' work, because it was different from many others when the anime first came out in 2009.
Seasons 1 and 2 are perfect as they are in the manga.
The characters have their strengths and weaknesses that perfect the entire anime itself.
According to the realistic way of things, they were portraying realistic reactions perfectly, according the reviews of the 2011 movie.
The realism of the anime was flawless because of how they portrayed as real people, not disposable trope cogs in a corporate machine.
I think everyone in 2026 should have a rewatch of the original season and season 2, because it will bring back the original fans of the show who still love it to this day. I hope I would recommend this to everyone.

Uh, I don't even even know where to start. Honestly, I’ve never seen a more beautiful anime in my entire life. I just know that Naruto Shippuden is gonna stay in my heart as my absolute favorite forever. I’m already so hyped to start Boruto because I just need to see how everyone’s lives keep developing. Shippuden hit me so hard emotionally that I can’t even put into words how I feel right now. If I had to break it down, I was basically crying 80% of the time, laughing 15%, and the other 5% I was just straight-up mad at Sasuke (sorry not sorry, lol). Ihaven’t read the manga, but I’m actually so glad I watched some of the filler episodes, especially the Kakashi and Itachi backstories. They helped me understand the world so much more. Though it kinda sucks that they skipped one super important part of Itachi’s story from the manga... a friend who read it told me about it and it sounds so heartbreaking.

I 100% recommend this to everyone. If you’re someone who wants to skip the repetitive stuff but still get the vibes, you should at least watch these filler episodes: 144–151, 349–361, 451–458, and 469. For me, they’re essential!

Honestly, the way this story was written... it feels like it was made by a god. I’m gonna be obsessed with this for a long, long time. ✨

"FLCL" both walks and works only on a thin line between genius and insanity- No I wouldn’t even call it walking, it’s more like running and doing tricks on said line while increasing the pace with every episode. Truly achieved the perfect amount of weirdness, while somehow creating a world that makes sense to be lived in while indulging in it. The first episode hits you like a truck, but from that point on out you just go "oh yeah this is FLCL makes sense". This incredible weirdness accompanied by Gianaxs beautiful animation is just straight eye candy. The only gripe I will admit to have had with this animeis that...
I can’t really bring myself to care about the characters as much as I would’ve liked.

Probably mostly due to the fact that they're treated to sell the overall feeling of adolescence, (what at least to my understanding seems to be the main theme of this series) instead of being fully fleshed out characters that you care and think about even after checking this as completed.

Still a really good show tho, I recommend this a lot if you want to have a fun short adventure set in an interesting world.

anime_title: Boku no Hero Academia: Sukue! Kyuujo Kunren!
review_text:
The episode brings some explanations about Todoroki. I hadn’t realized yet that Bakugo is rational and analytical despite not seeming like it. I know it was already possible to conclude that during the first season, but this episode is what made it very clear. I found the capture plan very intelligent on everyone’s part, especially Deku’s, and the fact that the teachers didn’t act normally seemed like it wouldn’t make any sense at all, but it did. The fanservice was enjoyable, the episode is good, and I’m glad I watched it. I didn’t even know it existed. The references to Anime Fest (a festival that showcasesanime specials, trailers, and exclusive content for fans) are there because the episode was originally released at Anime Fest 2016, airing between the last episode of the first season and the first episode of the second.

It was very hard to find the episode to watch, I made sure to save it in the cloud just in case.

As someone who usually hates Isekai, I actually loved this one. I really enjoyed the concept of dropping a person with a contemporary worldview into the "dark world" of the Middle Ages. I love how the protagonist’s ideals clash with the current reality. He realizes that the realm he has to manage is a failure, so he uses the modern knowledge he brought with him—combined with the power of witches—to guide his kingdom toward a horizon that no one else can see because they are blinded by prejudice. I liked how this work handles these themes; it feels much more honest. It hits differently than Westernmedia, where social commentary often feels artificial, as if it’s only there to please an agenda or a sponsor, resulting in a hollow critique.

Characters
Regarding the characters, the protagonist is a bit "bland" and lacks personality. However, personality definitely isn't an issue for Veronica. What a character! She is dangerous, mysterious, and charming all at once—she radiates an unparalleled feminine energy.

Final Thoughts
It’s a real shame this anime wasn't finished due to conflicts between the production company and other stakeholders. I’d love to know whose pockets we need to stuff with money just to get a continuation of this show.

anime_title: Hazure Skill "Kinomi Master": Skill no Mi (Tabetara Shinu) wo Mugen ni Taberareru You ni Natta Ken ni Tsuite
review_text: Stupid self-limiting plot any% speedrun. It was terrible before the end of episode 1. The animation is horrible. The pacing is worse than I’ve ever experienced. It’s laughably bad. The dialogue, the animation, the “plot” — it’s as if it’s literally been slowed down to extend it to 12 episodes. Just don’t waste your time. I started leaving it on in the background, hopping in and out, but shouldn’t have even gone that far. I may have disliked other things more, been more frustrated, or disappointed by shows that are higher quality, better paced, and better written. Yet after watching this I feel nothing. It’s truly the worstshow I’ve ever seen.

anime_title: Love Live! Nijigasaki Gakuen School Idol Doukoukai
review_text:
Turning the iconic Tokyo Big Sight convention center into a high school is such an outrageous idea. It's hilarious how much the show leans into the bit, having the characters do normal school things in a space that clearly wasn't designed to be a school at all. Yet it and the wider Odaiba area make a unique, distinctive setting that's perfect for this installment of the franchise. As opposed to Love Live Sunshine, which was about a bond with a town few would have heard of otherwise, Nijigasaki is about coming together in an attraction-filled place many in the world have already been to andconnected with. The show's visuals capture this special area well, in all its weirdly spacious and vaguely futuristic glory.

The biggest departure from the Love Live that came before is that this time the club isn't competing as a group, but are each performing as solo idols. It's an interesting angle, one that's not too strange given the rising popularity of VTuber idols that operate somewhat similarly. I felt like the setup lended towards characters that were a bit less one-note and more well-rounded, since each had to express themselves on stage alone. There's a good message here too about pursuing your dreams in your own way without compromise, even while giving and receiving support with those around you.

Much of the season consists of character-focused episodes to get to know each member of the cast, with a wide enough array of individual struggles that you're likely to really relate to at least one or two of them. Personally I was quite moved by Rina's character, since her struggle with facial expressions and tone of voice is one I have as well. Seeing her really shine in her own unique way meant a lot to me, and the show does a good job at making a lot of these types of moments land pretty well.
 
Is this the show that will sell people on the Love Live franchise? Probably not--for that I would point people towards Sunshine instead. Overall the impact just isn't quite ther

anime_title: Nanatsu no Maken ga Shihai suru
review_text:
Reign of the Seven Spellblades was something i honestly didn't have that many expectations from ,so that's probably one of the factors of why i actually enjoyed this damn show so much.But that's hardly all there is to it. What surprised me most about this show was the writing.It's a honest to god good story. It's nothing that would make you say "wow" with the depth and intricate plot or anything,but it's more then enough.Not every story has to be.Of course a good story would never be able to function without good characters.And they don't fail to deliver.One big bonus is that characters don't just havetheir arc where they are relevant and then they fall off the face of the Earth.This is a huge step above most stories.And they aren't usesless background characters either.Perfect example for that would be Milligan. Of course the mvp of this series has to be Hibiya.But the MC Oliver is also a good character.I enjoy the approach that was taken with his revenge as well. Im tired of gloomy emotionless loser MCs that view everybody ,even geniune people who want to be their friends as tools for their goals.It was intresting once,not all the time. And it also makes sense ,since what the people they take revenge for would have wanted (especially in Oliver's case) is for them to be happy. He ain't just taking revenge,he's building himself a new life as well.All in a day's work i'd say.

Also,what this anime does that most others do is they stick to the spells and what they do.Other weaker shows would've made one spell have 50 different effects by now.Oh and the power levels of different characters stay consistent.And there's the obvious levels.Of course all these are bonuses of solid writing.

And since it's all praise for almost everything this anime does i gotta give my complaint as well,and the reason i can't give it a 10. Animation's mid af.And it matters when there's alot of battles to be had in this show. They clearly had a very good artist on deck from the samples.But t

anime_title: Sore Ike! Anpanman: Fuwa Fuwa Fuwaly to Kumo no Kuni
review_text: I found this and decided to watch it because it looked really cute. So overall first it was super cute and I loved the songs. I watched this in English and I thought the dub was great. Anpanman's voice was super cute and it reminded me of ash ketchum. Dokeen's voice was good too. So first the plot was ok it was kind of slow and boring in some parts it gets way better in the climax. I liked the beginning just the middle part was slow. The characters were ok I love anpanman and dokeen the little cloud girl flurry was kind ofannoying to me. Bakinman was a decent antagonist. The theme seems to be about growing up and responsibility I think also friendship. I think the themes were shown well. The animation was really good and I love the art style. Overall I recommend this if you like cute anime you'd like this. Its a 6 bc its kind of slow at parts.

Una historia increíble, momentos épicos, personajes entrañables y muchos giros que nunca te esperas. Un viaje que te hace pasar por todos los tipos de sentimientos, ya que tiene de todo : acción, misterio, drama, comedía y sobre todo las mejores relaciones de personajes que he visto. El amor, la empatía y la esperanza son partes del camino de estos personajes, pero también la tragedia, la traición, la pérdida y la venganza, lo que hace que los villanos realmente sólo sean personas que han sufrido mucho. Son personas que lo único que buscaban era la paz en un mundo tan lleno de desgracias como loera el mundo shinobi. Naruto habla de como sin importar que tantas cosas malas te pasen, la mejor opción siempre será levantarte y seguir adelante, conservando tu alma y tu corazón puros. Esa es la mayor fortaleza para tener la determinación de no rendirte y siempre buscar la luz incluso en dónde sólo parece haber oscuridad. Porque dónde hay luz, hay oscuridad, dónde hay bondad hay maldad, y dónde hay paz, hay guerra para poder mantenerla. El mundo es gris y los conflictos nunca dejarán de existir, pero siempre habrán personas buenas por las cuales vale la pena luchar, tal y como Naruto siempre luchó para salvar a la persona que más amaba 💜

Even though I haven’t read the original manga, Tokyo Ghoul √A was still somewhat enjoyable—but it definitely felt like a step down from what it could’ve been. The biggest issue is the story. The plot is all over the place and feels rushed, like it’s jumping from point to point without properly building anything. Because of that, a lot of the emotional weight just isn’t there. The only time it really hits is toward the end, where you finally start to feel something again. Kaneki joining Aogiri actually made sense to me. The idea of him getting stronger to protect the people he cares about is solid,and I liked that direction for his character. The problem is how quickly and messily it was handled—it never gets the depth or development it deserved.

Some side characters stood out more than expected, especially Koutarou Amon. He felt grounded, serious, and morally driven in a way that a lot of other characters didn’t. His presence gave some stability to the chaos of the story, which made him one of the more memorable parts of the season.

The ending had both good and bad parts. It wasn’t terrible—I actually did enjoy it—but it could’ve been way more impactful if the buildup was stronger. What I really missed was the psychological depth. Season 1 had that heavy, introspective feel, and √A just doesn’t go as deep as it should.

I already have a feeling I’ll like it even less after reading the manga, since I know this adaptation changed a lot. But even on its own, it’s still a decent watch—just not nearly as strong as it could’ve been.

The build-up of Bai Xiaochun as the big little guy of the Anti-River (River-defying) Sect is ruined by one single decree from heavens and he is now the "honored" hostage. The author being he is, getting turned on by having other characters of the series hate the main one being his hobby, again make opportunities for him to suffer. How many times has this guy already endured zero to hundreds? I neither expect "WHAAT??" jaw-dropping plot twists nor demand intricate social game dynamics (I could really appreciate non-elemantary level of dialogues) from cultivation donghuas, as well as power-ups that follows every life-threatening moment, expectedly, cause dullnessovertime, yet it was still intruguing (more like stimulating lol) to keep me hanged for the next season to come. IMO, if it was popular enough it wouldn't still have 8 points.

anime_title: Sono Bisque Doll wa Koi wo Suru Season 2
review_text:
"Dear My Dress-Up Darling, what a wonderful experience you’ve taken me on." This season was a bit of a letdown compared to the first, but nonetheless it was still great and enjoyable to watch. Even when it didn’t hit the same highs as Season 1, it still kept the charm that made me fall in love with the series in the first place. The chemistry and bond between Gojo and Marin is still the heart of this show. Their relationship feels natural and wholesome, and it’s honestly beautiful to watch. The way they support each other and slowly grow closer really makes you feel invested in them.At times, it even makes you feel a little envious of a bond like that, because it feels so genuine and hard to come by.

That said, my biggest issue with this season is how far it leans into the side characters. While some of them are interesting, it sometimes feels like they take too much focus away from Marin and Gojo, which is what most people are really here for. I found myself wishing there were more moments that developed Marin and Gojo’s relationship rather than spending so much time elsewhere.

In not way is this show bad at all it is a great watch and any romance or slice of life enjoyer can experience i would recommend to anyone. 

Until season 3 goodbye goober...

Zankyou no Terror is one of those series that leaves you with strong mixed feelings, because while it clearly has many strong elements, it also stumbles in key areas. On the positive side, the atmosphere is arguably its biggest strength. The direction gives the show a calm, almost artistic tone that easily draws you into the story. The depiction of Tokyo feels realistic and often quietly beautiful, enhancing the dramatic mood. At the same time, the soundtrack by Yoko Kanno is outstanding—melancholic, unique, and one of the strongest aspects of the series, to the point where many scenes work largely because of it. The initial premise isalso very appealing: two teenagers acting as “terrorists,” leaving riddles and challenging the police. The early episodes build intrigue and create expectations for something deeper and more complex. The main characters, especially Nine and Twelve, have an interesting dynamic, and Lisa’s presence adds a more human and vulnerable layer to the story.

However, where the series seems to promise a lot, it doesn’t always deliver. The biggest issue lies in the writing. Many events rely on coincidences or plot conveniences, which weakens the sense of realism the show tries to establish. The characters’ actions—especially their motivations—are not always fully explained or convincing, reducing the emotional impact of the story.

Additionally, while the themes of terrorism and the protagonists’ past could have been explored in greater depth, they are handled somewhat superficially. The revelations about their backstory come relatively late and don’t always have the impact one might expect. Some characters, like Lisa, start with potential but aren’t fully developed, while others (such as the antagonist) create more confusion than tension.

Overall, the anime is a strong example of “style over substance.” It is well-directed, atmospheric, and musically excellent, but in terms of storytelling and character development, it falls short of its potent

I have a sentimental relationship with Full Moon o Sagashite. I bought all the manga volumes as they were coming out and enjoyed the series immensely. I still own those manga volumes to this day and have no intention of getting rid of them. However, my attempts to watch the anime didn't pan out. I only made it to episode 10 before stopping due to other obligations like school. It wasn't until this year that I finally managed to complete the anime, and only because AnimEigo licensed it for a complete blu-ray release for the first time ever. Viz Media once attempted to release theseries on DVD, complete with an English dub, but low sales made them stop halfway through, and many thought the second half of the series just never got dubbed, or if it did, it'd be lost forever. Imagine peoples' surprise when it was revealed that not only was the full series actually completely dubbed, but the second half of said dub would be on the new blu-ray! With the fact that the anime is now back in the limelight thanks to this news, I figured it was high time I got off my ass and finally watched the entire series. Though having knowledge of the manga, I do feel the anime doesn't really measure up.

The core premise is the same as the manga: 12-year-old Mitsuki Koyama has lived a hard life. Her parents died when she was little, and she spent most of her life in an orphanage. Two years ago, her friend and childhood crush, Eichi Sakurai, got adopted and is living in America now. Mitsuki dreams of becoming a singer, if only so she can reunite with Eichi. Unfortunately for her, she has a cancerous tumor in her throat called a sarcoma, which makes it hard for her to both sing and talk loudly, and the only way for it to be removed is through surgery, and even then, the procedure could make her lose her voice. One day, she's visited by two death spirits, Takuto and Meroko, who are surprised she can see them. Mitsuki is informed that she has one year left to live. Mitsuki decides to goad them

After finishing NHK, I'm left with very mixed feelings that lean slightly positive, but with quite a few reservations. The anime has genuinely strong moments, yet at the same time it feels inconsistent and at times even unfocused in what it tries to convey. Starting with the positives, the soundtrack stands out as one of the better aspects. It is simple but memorable and fits the atmosphere well. Another thing worth praising is the attention to continuity in small details. When something happens, it often has visible consequences in later scenes. It adds a sense of grounding that many series lack. The problems begin with tone andstructure. The anime tries to balance psychological drama with comedy, but the execution does not always work. There are moments where serious themes such as manipulation or loneliness are undercut by humor that feels out of place rather than complementary. The MLM arc is probably the clearest example of this. It had the potential to be meaningful, but it ends up feeling tonally inconsistent.

Pacing is another major issue. The beginning is engaging, but the middle portion slows down significantly and can feel like filler. The final episodes then speed things up too quickly, which creates a sense of rushing through important developments. The ending tries to tie things together, but it does not feel as earned as it could have been.

My biggest problem, however, lies with the characters, especially the main protagonist. His behavior often feels inconsistent. At times he shows extreme social anxiety, while in other similar situations he functions relatively normally. It is unclear whether this is meant to reflect psychological instability or simply a lack of consistent writing.

Regarding the portrayal of hikikomori, the series leans heavily into an exaggerated and sometimes caricature-like depiction. Instead of presenting a spectrum, it focuses on an extreme version, which makes it harder to relate to, even for viewers who might otherwise ide

Heard it was one of the best shows of the year and the comments under every episode were 'peak, I cried, rewatching'.. etc and all I thought after every episode was 'are we watching the same show?'. I watched both parts together and would say the first half was 'better' but I had to force myself to watch it. I think it had potential and the music carried it but the depth was lacking as if it was a poor adaptation the studio had scheduled on the side and just needed to quickly adapt yet somehow it seems to be fairly highly rated like anAkane Ga Kill of the next generation, maybe if I was a kid again I would feel differently, but as a 24y.o it does come accross as a childish and poorly made show I wouldn't reccomend to anyone I know irl.

Its a classic 90s OVA that doesn't really flesh out the story given how its only has 2 eps. Its quite good for what it is; a simple setup with cute ninja girls. The downside is that we don't really know about the characters, their background and their motivation, but i'm willing to look past that since again its a 2 ep OVA. Characters' personalities are really fun and lovable. The comedy aspect is quite good. The fight scene is surprisingly well made and really enjoyable. I would recommend this anime for people who just wanna watch something simple and fun. It doesn't have agrand story with fleshed out characters, but it does have a fun, action-packed fight scene of cute and goofy ninja girls.

In the vast sea of fantasy and isekai shows, it can be tough to know where to start. Delicious in Dungeon is not my first fantasy anime, but it honestly might be the best one I've seen. There is SO MUCH more to this story beneath the surface. The main cast are overall pretty funny and goofy, my favorite being the MC Laios. He is pretty quiet, but when he does talk it's usually hilariously silly. Marcille usually serves as the person to say "Oh no that's gross", but she is also quite amusing and caring. Chilchuck is the necessary straight man of the group; heis very cynical and a little obnoxious at times with his snide comments. Senshi is a straight man to a degree, but also has some moments where he tweaks out over food. Overall, there is real depth to all of these characters; they are deeply connected to the story, and it really starts to pick up about halfway through.

Let me tell you the truth about dubbed anime: Once you find a good one, you cannot go back. (For that anime) Personally, one of the best reasons to watch a dub is to hear the slang and nuanced voices in a language you understand. This show made me laugh out loud many times, precisely because of the voice acting. We also are kinda forced to watch in sub usually since that is the only available version at launch most of the time. I've seen the word "offbeat" used to describe Dungeon Meshi, and it definitely fits. There are a few instances where the story breaks narrative trends and does the thing we always wondered what might happen instead.

The writing, stakes, and themes are all very sound. Dungeon Meshi may appear to be a simple gourmet show about dungeons, but in reality, it's a fantasy juggernaut packed with a side of humor and cooking. I am going to need to read the manga ahead of season 2, which will likely finish the series, thankfully its not very long. Thank you for the meal!!

I don't even know what genre of anime this is. I only started watching it because I thought the idea of an anime girl having a gun with funny. I neither read the plot or knew the English title to this anime. I liked the beginning of the anime. All the characters felt quite complicated with their own motivations, both working against one another and towards their own goals. The world and worldbuilding was believable. It's surprisingly dark. It's more similar to the like of other philosophical animes like Evangelion. The one negative is that I felt like it was rushed.

This show was simple yet so complicated, heavy yet so light. It contradicts itself right, normally that would turn you away or even makes you question the writers skill. Ikoku nikki takes that in form of relationship with our main pair and shows you how it can happen. Show also very on point with everything, doesn't play around tells you right in to your face and moves on. It throws you around different timelines, imaginary places, hard subjects but as a whole everything feels natural. Simple animation and complicated story pairs very well because of that(i did it again). So if you want to wander about contrarythings. First think, think again, stop thinking and give this show a watch.

I previously had the earliest submitted review for this series. Owing to a lack of interest in anime for a long time I deleted my previous account, but feel that I probably should not have if just for the sake of the review I had done for this series. So the only thing I can do now is submit one again. This is not a perfect series by any estimation, but it has both ambition and a relatability that I think is exceedingly rare. The protagonist, Jun Fudo, is a woman who is uncertain of herself and what she truly wants. Underneath she is troubled,and one fateful night she is forced to discover to her horror that she has a mutant alter ego that all this time has wanted to surface. Forced to fight others like her by the mysterious Lan Asuka and the cold organisation she represents, Jun is constantly in fear of losing her humanity. Her inner conflict is a common one. We've all had moments in our lives where we've had thoughts and feelings of aggression that we are not proud of. Jun is presented with a situation where violence seems to be the only answer, and she is the one forced to inflict that violence on others at the behest of a cruel and calculating task master.

The situation endangers those around Jun and strains her relationships. She has a close friendship with a younger peer, Kazumi Takiura. She harbours feelings for her agent, Tatsuya Yuasa. Her attempts to continue with her work as a fashion model is often disrupted by her new duties; over time Jun realises there is no part of her life that can escape the taint of her bestial inner self. All the while she fears that the lust for violence she feels underneath is the mark of her true self than the outwardly gentle disposition she tries so hard for. Jun is by far the best aspect of this story, and carries the workload for the series admirably. My only complaint is I wish she was a bit more assertive in the second half of the story than she is.

I cried... I’m not really the type to get all choked up. There’s only a handful of movies or shows that can actually make me tear up. But... But this anime made me cry like a child. Beautiful strory, beautiful characters, beautiful art style, beautiful animations, beautiful musics. Everyting about Your Lie in April is, beautiful. That story really hit different. It’s clear as day that they reverse-engineered the whole story with a lot of thought. No character is just filler, everyone has a purpose and they all balance each other out perfectly. The art style, the palette, the visuals... everything is so gorgeous, it was a total feast for theeyes.

The animation was more than enough, but honestly, when it blended with the art style, it became a total masterpiece.

The music... man, the music was music to my ears. Everything from the OPs and EDs to the background score—and even the heavy hitters like Beethoven and Mozart—the whole soundtrack was straight fire.
For the first time ever, I actually want to listen to every single OP and ED. Aside from a couple of exceptions, I usually skip the endings, but with this one, I’m even vibeing with the background tracks.

Go and watch this masterpiece!

This is review of the Monogatari series as a whole. Venturing deep into Egyptian deserts in the 4th and 5th Century you will find monks such as John Cassian battling with vices, emotions and passions, which are in turn identified as spiritual creatures: demons, or spirits. The monks engage in conversations with them and try to identify the deep psychological causes and mechanisms causing them. Many of them are sexual passions. A type of psychotherapy, early pastoral care and spiritual guidance is born. Japanese spirit(ual) psychology is fascinating, because it identifies a parallel word. Psychological malaises or untreated wants and desires manifest as spiritual creatures, bakemonos. Thereis a way of dealing with them spiritually, or they can be overcome psychologically. Identifying the creature and engaging in conversation with them gives hints on how the creature can be defeated, or sometimes it will be transformed and become your ally. Reminds of C.S. Lewis' take on sexuality in The Story of the Little Red Lizard.

Monogatari series is a deep exploration of human psyche, wants, and sexuality, or if one will, of the spiritual creatures that inhabit this area. I had many realizations during the watching of this series. It is not for those who flich at the first sight of fan service and panties or bras, but for those who recognize even loli Hachikuchi as a sexual creature herself. The persons in the series transform from puppets into actors, regaing their agency through series of trials that bring about self-recognition and self-knowledge. Extremely good series, if you can digest the dark stuff in it, although the quality of different side stories and arcs varies. 

Overall: 9, different arcs: 7-10.

I really don't like battle shounen. And I like mecha even less. However, a fansub group had bothered to make a fan translation of this old anime in my obscure language and took its name from it, so I thought it might have something in it. Expected battles in some apocalyptic wasteland, and perhaps some buried mystery akin to Fallout. The vaults, that is, the underground villages, were explored less than I had hoped for, but the backstory managed to surprise. So wow. Just wow. This anime managese to surpass itself, and then surpass itself even more, and then surpass itself even more, multiple times again. Indeedto drill through the ceiling. And there is also laugh factor and memorable quotes, as the intensity is insane.

It's not good per se, but the wow factor and jaw opening factor is impressive. A bit like Evangelion. Worth watching, although not very deep. But manages to carry all its posturing with honour. 7/10.

Nisekoi is a rom-com, school harem anime that follows Raku, a high school student who happens to be the son of a Yakuza family, and his encounters with Chitoge (the daughter of a mafia boss), Onodera (a girl from a bakery family), and Marika (the daughter of a police chief). For any rom-com fan, you’ve probably experienced Nisekoi at some point and can notice a big difference between the rom-com market back then and now. Nisekoi puts a huge amount of effort into its comedy, and the way it builds character relationships through the “lock and key” promise is especially unique and engaging. The opening andending themes are great, the character designs are outstanding, and the animation and backgrounds have that distinctive, familiar style that Shaft brings to the table.

I haven’t read the manga yet, but Tokyo Ghoul Season 1 stands out immediately because of its premise and the dark psychological questions it explores. It hooks you very quickly, especially with the sharp transition from a light, almost positive beginning to a sudden and brutal turning point after the incident. That shift sets the tone for everything that follows. Kaneki’s struggle is easily the strongest part of the season. The way he slowly loses his sense of identity and starts questioning what it even means to be human is portrayed really well. He begins as a fragile, ordinary person, but under the right circumstances, you cansee him gradually break down until his entire way of life collapses by the finale.

The world itself is also very engaging. The dynamic between humans and ghouls, especially the idea of a food chain, adds depth to the story. At the same time, the show makes it clear that monsters aren’t limited to ghouls—there are humans in positions of power who are just as cruel, if not worse.

The impact of many scenes comes from the different perspectives we’re shown. Humans hate ghouls, but ghouls are simply trying to survive, which creates a constant moral conflict throughout the story.

The music is one of the standout elements. The opening theme and overall soundtrack add a lot of emotional weight and elevate key moments significantly.

The pacing is well handled, allowing Kaneki’s transformation to feel gradual and believable as he slowly loses his humanity and becomes what he’s forced to be.

So I watched Bubble — a 2022 anime film by Wit Studio that takes the story of The Little Mermaid, drops it into a post-apocalyptic Tokyo overrun by gravity-defying bubbles, and wraps the whole thing in some of the most gorgeous animation I've seen in a while. On paper, that lineup of ingredients sounds like it should produce something unforgettable. In practice, it produces something that's half unforgettable and half... fine. Let me start with what actually works, because it works hard. The Animation Wit Studio, clearly decided this film was going to look extraordinary, and they followed through on that promise completely. The parkour sequences alone areworth sitting down for. Bodies cutting through bubble-thick air, jumps that hang a beat longer than gravity should allow, the whole flooded skyline of Tokyo rendered with real weight and texture. There's a particular moment where a character's finger dissolves into foam mid-touch, quiet and unhurried, and it genuinely stopped me. No dramatic music swell, no slow motion — just something beautiful happening simply. That's the kind of confidence in visual storytelling that you don't see often.

The color work is also worth mentioning. Action scenes burn with saturated, almost aggressive color. Quieter moments cool down into softer light. The film knows what mood it's in and dresses accordingly, and the result is that every scene feels considered rather than accidental. There were shots that looked less like animation and more like paintings that someone forgot to stop moving. Captivating is genuinely the right word for it.

The Story
Here's where things get more complicated.

Bubble is built on a premise that has real potential — bubbles rain down on Tokyo, shatter gravity, render the city uninhabitable, and the only people left inside are orphaned teenagers competing in parkour tournaments to survive. That's a fascinating world to drop a Little Mermaid retelling into. The problem is that the film builds the walls of t

anime_title: One Piece Movie 03: Chinjuu-jima no Chopper Oukoku
review_text:
Watching this movie in the moment, I thought to myself “well, this isn’t good, but it’s not as bad as everyone says it is.” Then, I finished the movie, gave myself some space, and decided: it’s not the worst thing I’ve ever seen, but it certainly isn’t fun. And a One Piece story that isn’t fun is the worst kind of one piece story. The animation is fine. Nice colors, decent fight choreo, and some interesting shots. The OST is still a One Piece soundtrack, so I have neither complaints or praises. Once again, the Strawhats go to a random island, they fuck around, thenkill the local tyrants. Except this time, we’re in the Jungle Book/Tarzan of the Apes, and it’s a Chopper-focused adventure. And while I appreciate other members of the crew getting the spotlight, he is easily one of the worst parts of this movie. 

Between picking him up in Drum Kingdom and now, Chopper has spontaneously developed a fear of being hurt. This is an interesting quirk for him to have--he’s the rookie of the Strawhats and youngest of the crew, and a doctor whose goal is to cure all wounds. It’s interesting to have the rookie doctor come face to face with death, something he’s actively trying to prevent. 

But this doesn’t make any sense, considering he beat the hell out of Wapool’s lackeys bask in Drum Kingdom, pushed to near death himself. You could even argue that because of Vivi’s absence, this takes place after Alabasta, another adventure where Chopper got the snot kicked out of him and still persevered. Hell, he has a whole arc in Alabasta where he learns to do “the best he can” from Usopp and Nami. He shouldn’t have these problems!

To the movie’s credit, while Chopper acts like he is afraid of getting hurt, when he’s faced with combat situations, he doesn’t back down or cower. But he does do a hell of a lot of running away. Chopper’s flees aren’t cowardly in nature, and when he does flee in the movie it’s usually for a reason, but fleeing isn’t his only option--for god sakes, Chopper ha

I won’t lie—I was really looking forward to this movie. After finishing the manga, I dove into the film *On the Edge of Tomorrow*, which was out at the time. I’d say it stayed pretty true to the manga until the very end, but the happy ending itself left me pretty disappointed. So, when the Japanese announced an anime film in 2025, I couldn’t have been happier that I’d finally get a more faithful adaptation, and then D-Day came when I watched it and asked myself, where’s the problem? What happened? It was supposed to be better—after all, the Japanese made it; they couldn’t havemessed it up, right? Right? Apart from the title and the characters’ names, it has nothing in common with the original. They literally swapped the characters. In the manga, we start with Keiji and see how a weak soldier turns into a killing machine, and Rita doesn’t appear until halfway through. In this movie, the entire plot is based on Rita as the strong one and Keiji as the weak one, whereas in the manga, they were both the strong ones. And the ending? The same kind of happy ending you’d expect from the Americans. I’ll forgive the artwork for its wild style, and the character design and animation are on a high level since this is a movie and not TV, so they had plenty of time to work on it.

But I’m pretty disappointed with the movie itself, just like I am with the Hollywood version. I definitely recommend the manga.

anime_title: Boku no Hero Academia the Movie 2: Heroes:Rising
review_text: I recommend watching it between S5 and S6 of the anime. While the story probably takes place between S4 and S5 (Hawks is well-known), it unintentionally spoils the impact of a an event that occurs towards the end of S5, by doing something similar in the movie. Don't watch it expecting great plot; watch it if you want to enjoy some more hype with class 1A; especially if you enjoy seeing them synergizing their abilities together. This may have been the first time they've done it as a whole class against a common foe. Great opening, then slow build-up, but starts the action early in the movie(similar to pacing of the Overhaul arc). Visuals during the action are great, and great re-use of the anime soundtracks ... except until the winning attack. They chose an emotional score rather than hype action, and I think I know why, but it didn't work for me because we all know the loss they were foretelling wouldn't actually happen (it gets reversed because of plot convenience to be back in canon). This loss involved a fan-fictiony choice, which is a nice "What if", but I think was controversial

It's hard to ignore the fact that this is currently the highest-rated anime on MAL. Ignoring recency bias, this means that Frieren is considered the absolute peak of the *medium*, and will likely remain as such for a long time. At the time of writing this review, 469589 people have rated this a perfect 10 out of 10. That's half a million people. Holy shit. I don't watch a lot of anime (as you can probably tell by my list). I started 'seriously' watching anime in my mid-20s, and it's a little embarassing to say now that I deliberately chose the most artsy and obscure stuffto try to get into the medium (as you may also be able to tell by looking at my list), because I genuinely thought that my taste was fucking superior to those 'anime cretins' - or something along those lines. I was extremely dismissive towards all kinds of mainstream trends in anime even though I never experienced any of them first-hand. Suffice it to say that I had a pretty bad time with the obscure stuff I saw, but of course I never admitted that I may not have been very fair to the medium in the first place.

I did develop a more positive attitude towards anime over the last few years, and I've seen my fair share of not-too-obscure stuff since then. I would even go so far as to say that I liked some of it. But despite my change of heart, I walked away from the highest-rated animated series of all time wondering if I could ever truly fall in love with an anime, because even though I liked Frieren and have no negative feelings towards it, it left me completely cold. Pun intended.

It's not bad, mind you. It looks gorgeous, the animations are amazing and even smaller details are usually given a lot of attention. The story is told in a very laid-back and casual way, matching the personality of its main character. All the main characters are very likeable and the interactions between Frieren, Fern and Stark are often funny.

The first episode is genuinely intriguing, being set *after* Himmel's journey

-- From the perspective of a fan: I was waiting for the 11th episode, and just learned that's all there'd be for this season. The ending was abrupt and left a lot of implications in the air for what would come next. Normally, this kind of cliffhanger doesn't bother me (within a season or at the end). But the pacing matched the first season, which is to say that there wasn't much progression or development to the story here in just these 10 episodes. The initial response was a sinking feeling and sadness that almost brought me to tears—we'll probably be waiting another 3 years for thecompletion of what felt like an incomplete "season".

-- Story (8/10): What is here is exactly the quality you'd anticipate. Due to the slow pacing of the show, there just isn't much here. I'd hardly be compelled to even call this a "season". It felt like a half-season and ended as if mid-way through. It feels distinctly incomplete. This should have been released with the "third" season. Having it all together would have been spectacular.

-- Characters (10/10): The characters are written in an authentic, full-scope manner that make their problems and complaints immediately relatable. Both the positive and negative aspects of main characters' psyche have an impact on the story and their social dynamics. Frieren's social blind spots are directly related to her excessive lifespan—this manifests as deadpan reactions or a wise, placid contentment. But this seeming maturity is contrasted with her childish aversions towards judgmental behaviors from her party, or her tendency towards poutiness when she's forced to compromise on her greedy desire for grimoires. Similarly, Stark and Fern have their own blind spots (with some overlap). Overall, the star characters of Frieren are all believably and thoughtfully written.

-- Soundscape (10/10): The voices are clear and well balanced with the scenes. No muddiness or clipping. The scenes and atmosphere are complemented well by each soundtrack (which

anime_title: Kao ni Denai Kashiwada-san to Kao ni Deru Oota-kun
review_text:
At first, I thought Kao ni Denai Kashiwada-san to Kao ni Deru Oota-kun would be your typical slice-of-life with a slow, continuous progression. But it actually feels more like a series of short moments—each scene stands on its own, focusing on small interactions and then moving on. Surprisingly, that’s what makes it so enjoyable. It’s light, straight to the point, and never drags. I expected it to feel kind of empty, but it stays consistently entertaining all the way through. The dynamic between Oota and Kashiwada is really cute. Oota is expressive and shows his feelings by teasing her, while Kashiwada stays completely deadpan. But that contrastis exactly what makes it work—everyone can tell they like each other even if it’s never said out loud.

The humor is technically repetitive, but it doesn’t feel stale because their friends help keep things fresh with their reactions. And even though it seems static on the surface, their relationship still feels like it’s slowly progressing.

Overall, it’s simple, light, and a bit different—perfect if you just want something easy and genuinely fun to watch.

At first, I went into this anime with pretty high expectations. The premise was already strong—orchestral music, family trauma, a main character dealing with a heavy past. It felt like it had everything going for it. And early on, it really did feel promising. Aono’s trauma was treated seriously, not half-baked, so I thought, “okay, this is gonna go deep.” But the more it went on, the weirder the direction felt. People might call it a slow burn, but this isn’t the kind that builds anticipation—it just gets exhausting. It’s constant practice, some interactions here and there, but it feels like it’s just going in circles. The characterdynamics are also really awkward. Just when something starts to click, it shifts away. Then it comes back later, but by then the momentum is already gone.
The most frustrating part is how Aono’s trauma is handled. It was built up to feel really heavy at the start, but the resolution is just… that? He just accepts it? That easily? It ends up feeling trivialized, even though it’s supposed to be the core of the story.
And as a music anime, I was really looking forward to the performances. Instead, it’s the same practice scenes over and over, with the same piece repeated until it gets tiring. Then when the competition finally happens, they don’t even play that piece. Like… what was the point of all that buildup?
On top of that, the CGI during the instrument scenes is pretty noticeable and can be distracting. Those moments are supposed to feel immersive, maybe even give you chills, but instead they kind of pull you out of it.
By the end, there wasn’t a single moment that really made me go “wow.” It’s not a bad anime, but it feels incomplete in a lot of ways—and that’s what makes it more disappointing.
Because from the start, it clearly had a lot of potential.
But in the end, it just feels like: “this could have been so much better.”

I found that what really hooked me in Seihantai na Kimi to Boku wasn’t just the main pairing, but the subtle dynamic between Taira Shuuji and Azuma Shino. Their interactions feel understated yet naturally compelling, built through small gestures and quiet moments rather than overt romantic cues. Taira’s more relaxed and expressive personality contrasts well with Azuma’s reserved and composed demeanor, creating a kind of tension that feels genuine instead of forced. Without explicitly framing them as a couple, the story still leaves enough space for me to sense a growing connection, which made me unconsciously start shipping them. That organic chemistry, in my view,stands out more than the central relationship at certain points in the story.

I watched Kirei ni Shitemoraemasu ka, You can tune in and tune out, yes, it's one of those anime. You get what you asked for by reading the synopses and there is nothing more to it, no big reveal, no stakes, well almost no stakes and that is perfectly fine. The backgrounds are stunning and in my opinion alongside the soothing soundtrack the main reason to tune in. It's a Travelvlog ad in a way, you want to be there, and you kinda are. For 20 minutes you can relax, let the scenery pop in front of you and calm yourself down. The show takes potshots at a romanticsubplot but ultimately doesn't go anywhere. If you've seen similar shows this is familiar to you.
You WILL learn about cleaning and properly maintaining clothing articles, and some segments you can apply to your way to the washing machine and/or washerette. This approach to display hobby- or handywork activities, for the keen observer of this kind of genre anime, is infact not new either and will also be very familiar to you.

I watched Kirei ni Shitemoraemasu ka, and if you want to enjoy the gorgeous japanese seaside resort town and tropical get-away vibes and discover how you can properly maintain your laundry I believe you should too.

It’s not my strongest recommendation for sure, and for those who don’t enjoy a slower pace where the story isn’t driven by action it certainly isn’t for you. But if it does somewhat interest you I think it’s worth it to give it a watch, despite its flaws because there isn’t really another in the segment. It’s not life changing in any regard, but as an experience I think you would be better off having watched it than not, though it’s marginal. Despite being an anime about art, its actual art style is basic. That’s it, there’s no other descriptive word to use. However thatdoesn’t mean bad, just familiar. In the animation department it’s the same story. Characters in general are more interesting in their appearances than other shows who use this style though. Something I particularly liked was the face compositions. They’re not the basic anime ones, there’s more differences and in their features which makes them all more interesting. For me it raises the quality. There’s more thought put into the actual anime part than meets the eye. The actual works of art themselves are drawn well. It is impressive how they recreate oil painting looks with the tools the animators have.

The story gets started quickly, despite that there’s still (roughly) enough for 12 episodes. The story is paced for the experience (and not the viewing one), so as I said those into action won’t enjoy it, flat out. It’s a true slice of life. The writing is possessed with a certain depthless secular spirit in the lessons it tries to teach, which is disappointing but what can you do? It’s an anime about art club I suppose it’s to be expected. There is also a bit of “false advertising” surrounding Ryuji. Looking at the posters and art you might think he would play a much bigger role as he’s always beside Yaguchi. I suppose it’s actually better he isn’t though. On the topic of Ryuji, I found that the portrayal of his character was actually impressively accurate, though in the end the writers come to the “

anime_title: Isekai Mokushiroku Mynoghra: Hametsu no Bunmei de Hajimeru Sekai Seifuku
review_text: Among many isekais, and even among many video game Isekais, Mynoghra sits as one of the more interesting shows in the Anime space. Primarily focused around the premise of a hardcore (supposedly PVP) civilization builder game, the premise of the show is initially mundane: What if someone reincarnated into a world where they actually played out the process of developing the civilizations that grow in-game? The worlds top-ranked player of this game is ill and reincarnates into his favored civilization type, the evil Mynoghra Empire and begins working towards his usual play strategy, laying low and developing power. But the story is never that simple. Iwould highly implore anyone who is interested in subversions to consider watching this show blind. The switchup is not one which comes out of nowhere, it is cleverly hinted at through some events early on, but to see how it plays out here and how it will continue to play out hopefully in future seasons, this show is a very good exploration into the premise when it is made more complicated than it first appears. That being said, the majority of the content here is similar in essence to the kind of content found in, say, reincarnated as a slime, but with a more focused effort on the civilization aspect from the start. It may be boring to some, but it is heartwarming and heartbreaking at times, to the point where it is easy to get genuinely invested in the characters. I would say this is one of the premiere examples of an Isekai "power-building" show done right. Don't sleep on it even if it only has one season. I am confident what is here will satisfy most viewers tastes for those who can tolerate the Isekai premise.

This anime is about a single thing: struggle. Everybody has the choice of whether to wake up every day and face their own challenges, with varying degrees of foresight into what form they may take. Berserk '97 makes no statement as to the meaning of why people make this decision or their motivation. In fact, when the MC is asked why he continues to swing his sword, his response is essentially because I continue to exist. Therein lies the central thematic content: that to exist is to struggle against insurmountable forces which the individual has zero ability to enact meaningful consequence against. If this doesn'tspeak to you, I struggle to imagine the gilded fantasy in which you reside. Without stating specifics, the ending is the epitomal expression of this theme and, as far as I am concerned, is the point at which the source material should've ended as well. In terms of the execution in expressing this, it could at times be better, but perfection itself would be inhuman.

In the 80's, Mobile Suit Gundam catapulted anime in Japan. In the 90s, Dragon Ball Z broke the mainstream walls while Neon Genesis Evangelion opened the doors to the now indispensable late-night anime slots in Japan. In the 2000s, Fullmetal Alchemist marked the peak of manga-to-anime adaptations while Code Geass & Death Note headed the class of those who introduced the new anti-hero genre. And now, in 2013, Attack on Titan has shaken the industry once again. Unless you've been living under a rock for the past 5 months, you should at least know what the story is about, and for the sake of staying spoiler free, I'm notgoing into details on the plot.

Before I wrote this review, I wrote a different piece moments after watching the first episode stating that Attack on Titan had all the potential to become a landmark anime, knowing very little of how true that statement would turn out to be.

I have watched anime ever since I can remember. I have seen the very best and the worst that this industry has to offer. And I, like most of you, am very familiar with the relationship of manga-anime adaptation, which is the case of Attack on Titan. It is because of such experience and knowledge that I am able to say with so much confidence that Attack on Titan is the best anime series ever produced from any standpoint, at least, in the last decade.

Attack on Titan is such a well-done product that it has all the key strong points we all dream every series could have. By this I mean the story, characters, animation, OST, opening/ending sequences, art, directing, narrative, character development, and, most importantly, the "it" factor. These are all present with "A grade" production values. 

First of all, I want to start with the OST. Simply put, Attack on Titan's soundtrack rivals that of Rurouni Kenshin and Evangelion. Period. Don't misunderstand, they are not similar by any means, they are simply equally as masterful. Check on the internet the impressions left by fans about the f

Shingeki no Kyojin... Where do I start? In summary, Shingeki no Kyojin is a perfect anime to get somebody who loves action started on anime- I think it is literally the most mainstream anime ever made, and that is definitely not a bad thing. But with this comes downsides- ones not apparent to the viewer at first glance, but looking back on it after watching it around the time it first came out, definitely shine through. Shingeki no Kyojin starts off with a roar- the first 2 episodes explode right out of the gate with high-octane action that also leaves mystery to draw the viewer in. Whoare the titans? What is in the basement? Why is Eren's dad so creepy? All this and more the viewer asks themselves as the end credits roll for episode 2.

Then episode 3 and 4 jump in. It's a training scene for Eren and his friends, and it's dead slow in comparison. But that's ok- it's episodes like this that reinforce character development and get the plot rolling. And soon, the episodes with action will begin again, right? That High-Octane, bloody, gory action we've all been waiting for!

Well, it does. But then shortly after, it grinds back to a halt. Why do I bring up the first 5 episodes out of all things to mention about Shingeki no Kyojin? Because it perfectly frames the pacing of the entire anime. This would be fine and acceptable if it actually resulted in plot and character development going somewhere- but rarely this bickering between Armin and Eren result in anything productive.

Actually, Shingeki no Kyojin seems to show perfectly how to pull off the ILLUSION of plot and character development- Eren struggling with his inner demons despite somehow going back to square 1 every

Every once in a while, and even more frequently now than in the past, an anime appears that is completely and unequivocally shrouded in layers upon layers of hype and popularity from the anime community. It's the show that's on everyone lips, as well as everyone's TV screens. In the Fall of 2011, The Future Diary created an exceptional level of hype that had not been seen for quite some time. When the Summer of 2012 rolled along, it was time for Sword Art Online to take the spotlight, and its own popularity grew toheights far beyond that of The Future Diary. Then, in the Spring of 2013, we were treated to what one could consider the Game of Thrones or the Breaking Bad of the anime industry in terms of sheer popularity; a show that completed engulfed the entire anime community, dwarfing even SAO in popularity, but is this anime truly worthy of the hype that it received?

          Our story is set in an alternate universe where, 100 years prior to the events of the story, giant humanoid creatures known as Titans appeared and began consuming and decimating the human population, though with no apparent purpose for doing so. In response, the remainder of humanity built three enormous, circular walls to encompass their entire population and keep the Titans at bay. In this world, we meet three young children Eren Yeager, his adopted sister Mikasa, and their friend Armin, as they live out their peaceful lives in the southernmost city at the edge of the outer wall, Wall Maria. This soon changes, however, when a 60-meter Titan smashes through the main gate of Wall Maria, allowing Titans to freely enter the city and devour every human in sight, and forcing those who lived in the outer districts to retreat behind Wall Rose. During the chaos, Eren witnesses his mother being eaten by a Titan, and now he, Mikasa, and Armin have joined the military in the hopes of acquiring the skills necessary to take vengeance on the creatures that destroyed their lives.

Attack on Titan is a particularly unique case for me. As such, my review needs to be broken up to properly analyze it. Story: Attack on Titan has an absolutely astounding setting. The sheer idea behind the setting, the exposition pieces, and the initial plot elements held the potential for it to be one of the best action anime ever. From the first three episodes, I could see how someone could believe this would be that one anime that is just absolutely amazing. It simply becomes disappointing as things continue. Long story short? The start is absolutely amazing. It's powerful. It's grabbing. It does everythingan introduction should do. The problem after that stems from the plot slowly becoming painfully predictable and a personal dissatisfaction with the protagonists in place as well as the decisions of various members of the cast completely destroying the story's credibility at times. 

Art:

Art is completely subjective. I would not actually judge art based on someone's own opinion. You would really have to see it for yourself to deem whether it is "good" or not. As for me, it seemed fairly mediocre. The effort is there, but something with the art style simply does not jive with me. The settings are well done, but the characters are quite bland. 

Sound:

Finally. This is what I can praise Attack on Titan for. If anything, this series's soundtrack deserves to be remembered. It is excellent and its execution throughout the anime was not only strategic but tasteful. The tracks range from dark and depressing to powerful and moving pieces designed for a battle against a completely hopeless situation. In my opinion, the music in and of itself is the true beauty behind this anime.

I started watching Shingeki no Kyojin a year ago, almost to the day that I am writing this. So I figured I would write a review on what has grown to be one of my favourite series of all time. What I've noticed is that people tend to be very split about this anime; you either love it, or you hate it. Clearly, in my case, I love this entire series (the show, the manga, the side-stories, etc.) Now onto the actual review. Story: 9 At its core, Shingeki no Kyojin is something that we have seen many times in the past: humanity is being attacked, therefore wemust defend ourselves. Many, many iconic shows start from this format. However, SNK (as I will be calling it for the rest of the review) takes this simple plot point and expands it into something very, very unique. We have the Titans: giant, humanoid creatures set on devouring humans. Because of the Titans, humanity has been driven behind three walls, with the population dwindling to less than 1% of our current population. 

Each and every single plot point in this series is completely thought out and executed stunningly. There are so many twists that you won't be able to see coming. Hell, check out episode 8- you'll be shocked. Even the filler is thought out, and has important significance to the main story. The story of SNK goes even further in the manga. At the time of writing this review, there has been 59 chapters released, and we are just only starting to scratch the surface of this world.

Like most things out there, SNK does have its flaws. But the flaws are so minuscule that it's very, very hard to notice them. The brilliant storytelling of this series completely blew me away from episode 1, which is why it deserves a near perfect score.

Art: 9

There’s a current trend that favors dark and edgy stories such as Game of Thrones, The Walking Dead, and so on. Given the astronomical amount of hype behind the series it certainly had a lot to live up to. That said, the hype was understandable as Attack on Titan seemed to have everything going for it, being produced by Production I.G., having top notch production values, and a promising premise. Attack on Titan follows the story of Eren and the Survey Corpsas they fight for survival in a world where monstrous beings known as titans terrorize humanity.

I was very intrigued by the premise of Attack on Titan. It’s definitely something that stands out and is at least somewhat unique in a sea of all too similar anime. That’s sadly where my praise ends for the series. Unfortunately the execution does not live up to the premise and it's not long before the show turns into glorified torture porn.

The majority of the characters are bland and pretty cookie cutter. This is a huge problem in a series where death and survival are the main aspects, and a character’s death has no impact unless the viewer is attached to them. Without spoiling too much there are one or more important characters that are introduced and die within the span of a single episode. The scenes are supposed to be impactful, yet these characters are barely shown in the one episode they are in creating little emotional payoff. 

Eren has one emotion- anger, and only one way of expressing himself- yelling. Combined with his naive and arrogant attitude and inability to back much of it up, Eren can be a very annoying character. The show is incredibly engaging, and intense because the combat animations are executed perfectly, giving a really personal front row seat throughout. This anime made my mouth open wide for longer than 5 seconds more than a handful times. This is the type of world where more people get killed than the entirety of the A Song of Ice and Fire series. It exists in a timeless universe – not in the past, present or future of our world, but in a pocket universe where Titans existed long before the existence of machines. In this world, machines were not invented to improve humanity’s lifestyle, but for the continuous existence of humanity itself. There’s lots of shouting (especially from Eren), lots of action, lots of character development, and lotsof tears. My friends weren’t kidding. I felt like my ear drums would break from all the intense shouting. I think my mind is forever scarred, what with how everyone got crushed like ants all the time. And my heart has become immune to all other fictional deaths, what with all the deaths I’ve experienced just from this show alone.

It teaches you a lot about what it takes to be a soldier, a survivor. Constantly, people have to be reminded to “FIGHT!” Some lines are repeated all over the anime that it kind of becomes engraved into your head and heart once you’re done with it.

It’s the kind of anime that sticks with you, the kind that leaves you with phantom faces with phantom voices following you around, making you feel as if you’re still living in that universe, even after you’ve told yourself that you’re done with it.

The concept of the villain, and of the world itself, is scary and genius. First off, the villain – the Titan – is basically a stupid giant human cannibal.

Unlike other villains, it doesn’t want revenge. It doesn’t want world domination. It doesn’t want power. It doesn’t need to eat. It has lived a hundred years without eating humans. But it is all it ever eats. It doesn’t eat animals. It wants only one thing – and this is just a theory, but it’s one that I can side with, and one that I love the most – to annihilate the existence of humanity.

The hype of 2013 leading to an ending that closed off lots of action and questions throughout its run. With an anime that has a lot of hype, comes a lot of conflict between haters and people who genuine enjoy the anime. Shingeki no Kyojin is an anime that made peaks in sales and getting fans world wide, but is it really worth watching? The concept of humanity being at the brink of extinction, this is one emphasizes it's fight for survival and the ups and downs of their fight for victory. With the first episode starting off with a great clincher to get youexcited for the rest of series, you get a little view of how each character is and their view point towards the situation. 

Due to the story of humans being driven to extinction, you often get an anime that can be a hit or a miss depending on how they unravel it throughout the series. With the show being a fight for survival, it does an excellent job actually dragging out the battle to its actual length. With anime these days battle scenes are usually only an episode with the exception of it being mostly dialog. Attack on Titan has arcs of each fight against the titans or progress in the story. You're given arcs that focus on a battle to take back a city, an expedition outside the walls, and even a battle taking place inside their safest town. 

Throughout the anime you get stories of some characters past and they're definitely something that makes each character enjoyable and much more interesting to watch as the series progresses forward. The characters are mainly focuses on Eren, Mikasa and Armin. They're what drives the story and is the foundation for all the events that happen.

Shingeki no Kyojin is an amazingly written story with a very dark premise and a lot of action. This is definitely not a series for the faint of heart, especially those with fears of man-eating giants that don't even have the decency to kill you before they eat you (sometimes). The story keeps you guessing as it progresses, there's a new reveal that completely destroys what you thought you knew and makes you wonder just how far the rabbit hole goes. The characters are very relatable and you really do want to see them succeed. What makes this series unique however is the protagonists veryrarely ever win a battle. Every mission the cast engage in usually ends in a blood bath, but it's not without purpose. The gruesome deaths we witness just cements how high the stakes are in this story, and the determination of the protagonists to push forward is very inspiring.
Another unique thing about the cast is they are rather three-dimensional in comparison to a lot of anime and manga I've seen. Where I expect some characters to be completely flat jerkasses or emotionless bastards, there's always something that really makes you like them. The series also makes a point of establishing that no matter what you see on the outside, the characters certainly feel and act human deep down. This makes it really heart breaking when you see characters you like die in the line of duty, and only makes you want to see the good guys succeed all the more, which serves to make their victories so much sweeter.
tl;dr, This series is a must-read/watch. If you have a weak stomach or a phobia for cannibalistic slasher smiles, give it a miss. Otherwise, give it your full attention.

Humanity was driven to the brink of extinction. The few surviving humans built a society behind 50-meter walls that kept the Titans out. Our main characters, teenage boy Eren Yeager and his foster sister Mikasa Ackerman witness something horrific... The city's "divine" walls are destroyed by a colossal titan that appears out of thin air. As the smaller titans flood the city, the two kids watch in horror as their mother along with thousands of other humans are eaten alive. Eren vows to murder every single titan and take revenge for all of mankind. And so, mankind finds their secret weapon in defeating the Titans... 

First thing you may be wondering, is this anime as epic as everyone says it is, and is it filled with amazing action scenes? Well eh, I guess you could say that it's a tiny bit, not too much, but just a teeny bit ... IT'S DEFINITELY EPIC. It pains me in a way to not give Attack on Titan anything but a 10. I completely understand why this show got so popular so fast. I also understand why everybody is impatiently waiting for season 2. I want to say that it is really as flawless as people say it is. But I can't. While I would genuinely recommend this show, I am still a critic. I still have to acknowledge that this show has problems. As good as this show is, and it is very, very good, I cannot with a good conscience say that it does not have glaring flaws. The biggest isthe simple fact that this show has too much talk. I know that dialogue is crucial to telling a story and developing character, but there is a time and a place for it. There were several occasions where they have a really good story coming up in what should be the very next episode, but the show digs its heels into the ground and spends an entire episode talking about what was coming. It just seems like whenever this show can build up momentum, it feels the need to deploy the drag chute. This show is not nearly fast-paced enough. But this problem also appears within the action. There are so many five minute long conversations, or inner monologues, or flashback sessions smack in the middle of an action scene. The pacing of this show seriously needs work.
Thankfully, the good aspects of this show trump the bad ones hundredfold. The action is incredible, the art is distinctive, and the voice acting is some of the best that I have ever seen. Is it perfect? Absolutely not. But it is by all means worth watching.

What would happen if humanity was no longer on the top of the food chain? How would we adapt, and how would our perception of the world change? Tetsurō Araki came to answer these questions with the release of his latest series. It is a tale of tragedy and revenge; of uplifting hope and crushing sadness. A love letter to the struggle of humanity and its destructive nature. This is Attack On Titan. Setting: The exact location and time place of AOT is unknown, but based off of the clothing, architectural style and character names it is safe to say that theseries is located around Germany and takes place during the 17th century.  Humanity has locked itself away behind three impossibly tall walls named Maria, Rose and Sina.  After the outer most wall, Maria is assaulted and damaged titans flood in, starting our tale of woe.

Story:
The story of AOT is quite simple, but not in a bad way.  Yasuko Kobayashi (the anime writer) didn't over complicate the plot with half baked symbolism or conspiracy pandering.  AOT knows exactly where it wants to go and doesn't waste any time getting there.  Don't get me wrong I'm not saying the series is dull, far from it actually; I just find it refreshing to watch a show that doesn't spend the first half trying to convince about me how cool and artsy it is.  I knew exactly what I was getting, and AOT never changed that.  In addition to the narrative, the actual setting is wonderfully crafted.  While removed from reality AOT never strays to far from the believable.  Everything fits from a historical point of view; the powerful religious presence, the centralized political structure, the proper use of period weaponry.  The only problem with the story its pacing.
