This season is a great example of how a show with potential could rapidly descend to mediocrity, and this is coming from someone who enjoyed the first 2 seasons. The writing this season has been so bad, that it’s impossible for me to review it without dissecting just how bad every part of this season was, so spoiler warning. Anyone who hasn’t already seen this season, stay away from this review. We start off with a mini training arc, continuing this show’s incredibly repetitive formula of training arc – villain attack – tournament arc – villain attack – training arc – villain attack. Then we’re remindedof one of the show’s weakest points, the villains, they’re just so hilariously incompetent, they always lose, and they’re painfully dumb. Shigaraki, who after his confrontation with Deku last season, the show would like you to believe he’s some sort of a criminal mastermind now, came up with such a genius plan: kidnap Bakugo and turn him into a villain. Why Bakugo specifically you might ask? Did he do his research and discover he had a history with heroes that would make him more likely to betray them? Some psychological weakness he could exploit? Nope, it’s because he saw how angry he was in the ceremony of the last tournament and thought “Hey, he hates the hero society just as much as me!!....Yes, that’s right. That’s the reason he risked his entire crew (and lost some of them) for. Remember how it was weird when Todoroki suddenly didn’t want to use fire against Bakugo in the final even though he just had that break through with Deku and used fire just 1 episode earlier? I thought it was just because Horikoshi wanted to save this cool fight for later in the story maybe, the reasoning was lame but I didn’t mind. But now I get it, Todoroki didn’t use it, so Bakugo would be upset, so Shigaraki would see it and this whole stupid arc happens now. Brilliant writing.


Deku’s fight with Muscular made it certain for me that this show wasn’t as good as I thought it would be.

"I want to win like All Might. No matter what anyone says, that will never change!" - Katsuki Bakugou What does it mean to be a "hero"? Is it someone who saves those in need? Someone who defeats all the bad guys? Someone who brings good things to the lives of others? All of these? Or maybe something entirely different? Maybe it's all a case of perspective. A story about superheroes authored by mangaka Kouhei Horikoshi, it quickly rose in popularity and received an anime adaptation less than two years later in April 2016, animated by Studio Bones (FMA, Soul Eater, Noragami). Created in the middle of the western superhero age of media, it's no wonder that it also became a popular series in the West. As it stands currently, the third Season of BnHA is the most popular anime of 2018 on MAL. The following are my very personal and biased thoughts on this entry in the series.

"Next, it's your turn." - Toshinori Yagi

Story:

Following the events of the 2nd Season, the students of Class 1-A are on their Summer vacation after taking their finals. What starts as a fairly standard Summer break quickly escalates into the most dangerous situation the students of U.A have been a part of yet. The events of the first half of this Season have consequences of utmost importance to the world and characters of the series. Not only Horikoshi introduces unique new villains in the story, but also keeps building on its concepts of heroism and villainy by making the series' protagonists and antagonists clash with fists and ideals. The fast pace of the Second Season is also present here, which keeps the action engaging up until the climax of the arc.

In the second half, things slow down subst

Have you ever liked an anime's characters so much you wished they got out of the dreadful story they are involved in? Well, BNHA is this case. Why does a season 2 or a season 3 always get more praise than season 1? That's because people who already saw the awful plot already dropped the series and the only ones remaining are the ones who praise it a lot. But why do i keep watching it even though i almost gave Season 1 a 5? Well, because people told me Season 2 was a "masterpiece". I decided to give it another chance and I was notdisappointed.  Midoriya's plot armor had disappeared for a time being and the anime focused on other great characters (such as Todoroki and Ochako).  

As a matter of fact, I decided to give Season 3 a go.  Season 3 is the worst season of BNHA, and it's not hard to know why (spoilers ahead):

1) Plot armor.  Midoriya once again defeats a strong enemy (who nobody cares for btw) using an asspull power-up.  All Might defeats the most powerful of all enemies by just talking and a Smash, ugh.

2) Filler episodes.  They stretched the whole License Exam arc, one of the worst arcs out there. Playing dodgeball? Ugh.  Rescuing people? Ugh.  The worst of all filler episodes was the mystery episode.  Midoriya solved the case just himself, which takes me to the other point:

3) Midoriya.  He is becoming the Kirito of BNHA.  He solves cases, always saves his classmates, girls love him, boys praise him.  even the teachers always speak about him "he's the heart of the team".  And what even bothers me most, is that Midoriya can't stop crying.  He has to cry 2 times each episode.  It doesn't feel emotional at all, it feels horribly forced.  When Bakugo cried, it felt real.  Midoriya is just crying and crying for everything.  He is the worst character in this series.

4)Arcs and villians.  Have you noticed how we are moving in a full circle? That's because this story is not going anywhere.  We get a "training arc", then an "exam arc", then the villians appear

I really enjoyed the first two seasons of this show. But the characters and story have failed to remain interesting, so I will not be keeping up with it anymore. It is so clearly drawing out the story as long as it possibly can so the show can run for as long as possible. This season peeked dramatically half-way through, with a tired "win by punching harder" fight. And the rest of the season was pretty dull. Ultimately Another Forgettable Anime. What else do I have to say? In the first two seasons I fell in love with the characters and the setting, but this season failed to deliver drama,romance, or comedy. Although it did have a few new concepts I enjoyed.

STORY - 8 ART - 9 SOUND - 9 CHARACTER - 9 ENJOYMENT - 9 Plus Ultra, the Latin phrase we've come to love ever since the epic final showdown on the first season of Boku no Hero Academia. Meaning to go further beyond, it encapsulates the very definition of what Season 3 is all about. How far can the society of Heroes vs Villains run for without causing major destruction? What does it take to ensure protection, stability, and peace among a changing world full of Quirks? The story wrestles these questions solidly and seeks just how much can heroism triumph against those who wants to crumble society. Upon thevillains' ambush, justice treads over a dangerous tightrope. When more menacing personalities and powers arrive on the doorstep, the suspenseful atmosphere becomes a trigger that sends out a wave of harsh realities towards both students and pro heroes alike. This has now turned into a stage to protect. As new insights are revealed within the evolving villainy allegiances, characters step outside of their comfort zone to battle against destruction. Emotions whirl around, strategies and moves are formed to ensure the best way out, and heroic resolutions truly shine to guard society from the flames. All these mayhems not only add to just be bursting with tense goosebumps or powerful excitements, there's also the influential message of the symbolical justice passing down onto the next generation. Ultimately, they're the next ones who have to fight. And to transition with this succeeding group of heroes, additional focus is given to more hero schools out there. A showcase of developmental progresses and relationships are shown within the students of Class 1A as they face against other students. Their growth ever since the beginning feels very tangible now and it's this aspect that answers why they are deserving to become society's future heroes. They will continue to go further beyond.

My Hero Academia has done something that sets it apart from all other anime in the Shonen arena. The episodes excel in every category. It is written well in that there are good reasons provided for why characters act the way they do. Fight episodes contain engaging animations as well as purposeful dialog. The animation style itself is also enjoyable. They take advantage of crisper animations while retaining the anime feel. My one concern is that the writers made the My Hero Academia universe large enough to keep the series going for years to come! Right now it seems pretty focused on one main villain(and I don't expect it will be a show like Bleach that will open up entirely new words that come out of no-where to add seasons worth of content). I hope the show doesn't end when the known villains are taken out.
Overall, I thoroughly enjoy this show, it's definitely worth a watch. It has fun and compelling characters, and interesting narrative, and can even get really intense, at times. There are also a good amount of cute and funny moments to give a breather from serious topics, so it's definitely not a depressing anime. I'd compare it to Steven Universe in its coming-of-age+magical powers story along with One Punch Man's themes and the like moods that either show bring to the table, portrayed through a shonen anime style. With great animation quality and excellent pacing, this show has quickly made itself one of my top favorite shows that's currently airing. It might not be for everyone, but I urge you to give it a chance.

Unfortunately, I picked up the anime and manga series almost a year and a half later, watching the next thirteen episodes, and witnessed the truenature of My Hero Academia.

Most of our characters, after 60+ episodes, have almost no development. For the most part, they are not needed in any important situation, and the series could easily do without them existing at this point. I notice that the series has a habit of introducing new characters and making them SUPER important, but doing frickall with Class 1-A. My Hero Academia is a show that has a very interesting way of melding Western superhero culture (think Marvel/DC) with shounen & high school elements from the anime world. As I mentioned in a review I did for season 1, this show's ability to take inspiration from outside the anime world might be why it feels more refreshing than I thought it would. MHA has come a long way indeed since season 1, where I nearly dropped the series because I felt a bit overwhelmed at the size of the cast and saw nothing special. If season 1 was an introduction to the setting and Deku/AllMight, season 2 was a proper introduction to all the students of class 1A and began to explore their motivations and backstories. Now, in season 3, we got to see a continuation of that, and more rapid maturation of the characters through adversity.

SPOILERS BELOW

For the record, I think season 3 was the best one yet. HOWEVER, I simply don't understand why the apex of the season's storyline, and basically the entire show up to this point, occurred on S3 E11 (in case you don't remember, this was when we got to hear "UNITED STATES OF SMASH!"). That REALLY should have been the last, or second to last episode for maximum impact. 

Seeing that happen so early made the rest of the season (which was mostly filler anyway) feel so weak in comparison.

Other than the backwards structure of season 3, I feel that the story has definitely become less formulaic since season 1 with the whole idea of All For One and the removal of All Might's power. I think there are a lot of interesting directions the show can go as we head into season 4, and I look forward to more unique elements in this category.

I'm not sure if the art has improved or not, but I'm still impressed at the quality of the character designs and the excellent animation. I have no complaints whatsoever.

Sound was a bit more average to me, as I didn't hear anything particularly special in the OST or intro/endings.

It's undeniable at this point that the Boku no Hero Academia is at the pinnacle of its growth, with two successful seasons, popular Manga, new upcoming games, feature movies, and I cankeep on going for long, this show is definitely a highlight for the new generation of anime. Boku no Hero Academia 3rd Season continue the trend of its two previous seasons on being a seasonal show that doesn't fall short on delivering quality entertainment in all sorts of aspects, much like what Shingeki no Kyojin got us used to, crisp animation and phenomenal audio work were always a nice guy with open welcoming hands, doing a shounen feature justice for once.

Best word to describe this anime? Solid. Solid characters. Solid plot. Solid action. Maybe a little over-rated (OK considering the hype machine definitely over-rated), but still DEFINITELY worth your time. If you're looking for something that completely redefines the genre - this ain't it. It's not going to expand your thoughts on moral/philosophical concepts. It's not going to make you have these deep emotional connections to characters you'll find relatable. It's just fun. It's the kind of anime that anime snobs will turn up their noses at, but you can binge watch and walk away from it at the end without thinking you wasted your time (well, not anymorethan binge-watching any other show makes you think you wasted your time).

5.5/10 ~ better then S2, worse then S1 MHA was a show with a really strong start in S1 and then kinda fell off imo. S2 was pretty disappointing but S3 did step it up a bit. Which is funny because originally I dropped the show when it was airing around the time Slain got captured and all I recall hearing from m y peers about the show is about how bad Season 3 was and that they wish they dropped the show all together, in 2025 I dont know a single person that is still watching this seasonally because everyone dropped the show between S3-5.After watching all of Season 1-3, + the first 2 movies. I now understand what must have happened. MHA does something kinda well for a Shonen specifically which is mix the emotional aspects of the show, intertwined with the fights and level ups. But the emotional aspects of the show sit in this weird category where I feel like its too emotional for the general Shonen crowd (IE: the target demo that is Teen/Pre-teen boys) But is simultaneously not mature enough for the adult crowd that are probably now into seinen or w.e, not to mention too violent for kids, and tackling touch topics like morality for children.  I recall many people saying S3 was so "boring", after S2 that just jumped into a stupid tournament arch and featured the best boy Slain. Where S3 dives much deeper into character growth and the emotional aspect of the show with far far less fighting. 

But at the end of the day, its still a Shonen and im forsure in the "too old for this shit" category. I mostly enjoy the semi political aspects and the moral questions the show really does a poor job of asking. As I said in my previous 2 reviews if I didnt need extremely mid show to watch while working I would never have made it past the mid point of Season 1. 

That being said I think this show is pretty decent as a 1st anime, and probably great for kids between 10-16's

I wish I had more to add for this review based on the ones I did for the first two seasons, however honestly not much has changed from the second. While the second was definitely my favorite, this season was great in it's own right. The work done in the summer camp was a great way to introduce and flesh out not only the hero classes, but also the villains as well. Characterization of the villains is an often forgotten about story marker in anime (In my opinion at least), which doesn't always amount to sympathy either. Even some of the underlings have their own motivations,desires, and reasons for doing what they're doing. Some are influenced by Stain, while others simply live for chaos (a bit cliche, but I like some variety). Even then, the arc with All for One and All Might was about characterizing both of them and All for One's protege, not just All Might himself. Even Bakugo got some decent screentime that really fleshed out his frustrations with Deku, better than the previous two season.

This season felt a bit more like going through the motions than Season 2. While S2 still had arcs, the arcs for this season felt a little more disjointed for me. Even then, the characters made really nice progression and the sense of dread at All Mights waning strength and Deku's attempt to step into the role is palpable. Overall it felt more like a commercial for Season 4 but I'm on board.

Side Note: I did feel the Uraraka sideplot of her crush on Deku was only put in so that they didn't have to really talk about it for a while. I'm not sure if I wanted that only because I really like the side interactions characters have outside of class or work.

Of the three seasons I found this one the most boring, but I enjoyed it more then season one due to my growing love of the characters. The biggest downfall for this season is the lack of pay off for the two seasons of buildup. All for one is portrayed to be a mega-powerful villain, and he is, but he is somehow defeated very easily in a relatively boring boss-fight. This is redeemed however by Allmght and the internal battle he goes through during the fight. The test arc is pretty entertaining but not quite as much as the tournament arc from season 2. Myfavorite part of this season is the relationship between Deku and Bakugo which sees a lot of growth, especially in the Bakugo area.  You can tell how much Deku has grown as a character compared to season 1.  Deku used to be one of my least favorites, but now he is one of my favorites along with Mr. Aizawa.

One can say "oh it didn't hurt the overall season though" and to that I say "Ididn't hurt the overall season FOR YOU" for me if didn't make overall season better then leave it out. The only other thing from a story standpoint that I didn't necessarily like was the dialogue during the muscular fight scene which was controversial when that part came out in the manga and the mangaka even apologized for said scene saying something along the lines of "he could have written the dialogue in that scene better" which I thought he would have attempted to tell Studio Bones to change it but didn't oh well his decision and he's holding his L for it maturing and moving on.

In my opinion this is the weakest season of MHA so far. Boku no Hero Academia 3rd Season And here we come to the point where MHA, unfortunately, announces its downfall. Here the viewer again becomes terribly aware of how generic this series actually is and how little value is placed on variety and creativity. -Don't get me wrong, I'm still a big fan of the series and love every single episode, but you can't ignore something like this. This season begins with a training trip into the wilderness, so that the students of classes A and B, find out their own strengths and try to compensate fortheir weaknesses. But the villains know about this excursion and ambush them, resulting in a (not) epic battle. Perhaps it was better in the manga, but in the anime, its pacing was very slow and lacked momentum. The attack of the villains was not convincing at all.


What surprised me more, though, was their goal and what that resulted in. Although you might think that Bakugo is actually more like a "villain" than a "hero", here again, his will was demonstrated why he wants to be a hero after all. And that this fight resulted in the clash between All Might and All For One, no one could have seen this coming. Who builds in a fight against the final antagonist in the third season? This was indeed surprising, even if the course of the fight had been a bit stretched and lacked dynamics. Not to mention the final blow of All Might, cliché but convincing.


So far it wasn't actually that bad, it was "Average". But what awaited me after the fight was torture. By far the most boring and monotonous School Arc in the series. Not only was it uninteresting, but the pacing was also slow and there were almost no entertaining moments, even for me. If I could, I would have almost skipped this arc.

I feel this was a solid season. It introduced us to a lot of aspects about the world that were fun. A lot of quirks that were interesting to see in action, and characters that I hope are expanded upon later. It does get sloppy in the overall planning and character motivations departments that completely take you out of what is happening. The first half of the season is very strong. It answers some great questions about the world and introduces some great character arcs for the main character to have achieved. Him actually saving someone and being fulfilled by it is a cozy littlearc that also bled into the character he was saving in very uplifting ways. The expansion on the MC's mother was very welcome. She acts very much like a mother and the iteration between All Might and her was very emotionally satisfying. Every character that is expanded upon acts in very real and emotionally relateable ways. This leads into one of the most detours of the series where it greatly explains a lot about All Might and the arch nemesis that the previous seasons where debating. The climax is very emotional. You will most likely know how it ends, but it is one of the best shonen moments because of how the character of All Might was built up for so long.

The problems start to arise in the second half. Not entirely because of how it is built, but the major character motivations and results. A lot of it stems from not following the very first line it lays down in the series about no one being equal. For example, it feels it has to always get every single character from the class to the same point. It undermines what it wants to do about showing some of these characters fail. A lot of deus ex machina or saved by the bell types of scenarios that save characters who failed from their failures or character flaws. So when you know every single character is going to get to a certain point then there is no tension, especially after the first arc in this season. Because of the first arc and then

Although many consider this season worse than the previous one, I do not think it is like that. The animation, at times, may have seemed a bit worse, especially in important scenes like AFO vs OFA, but it generally remains in line with the previous season. While in the previous season we focused on character development, in this season we focused more on world development (although there is still character development). The world of the series evolves and in a realistic way. There are new characters and an interesting development. It is still a shounen series, so its story will not be totally incredible, butthis is not necessarily bad, and if you like these series, you will enjoy it. Recommended.

The third season of Boku No Hero Academia was kind of a bummer. I don't remember if I felt this way about previous seasons, but this time around the pacing was awful. I think the show is too ambitious and not enough happens per season. During the training camp arc, their focus was just all over the place. Like that arc had some amazing fights in it... but there are too many characters. Taking the time to zero in on each of their quirks and to show their growth/fighting just really bogged down the series for me. Like the chaos that broke out with thevillains descending on the camp? In real time that fight was probably less than an hour long but it was SO MANY EPISODES and not enough actually happened per episode!!! The highlight of that arc was Midoriya's fight with Muscular because it definitely still had his emotions during the fight, but there were less cut scenes with him being deep in thought and more action instead. 

Although this won't happen, I think the series would benefit from axing 3/4 of the characters. Focus on the main students (Midoriya, Todoroki, Bakugo, Uraraka, & Iida) ...I'd even settle for just the first three to be honest)), a few of the pro heroes/villains and the story would be infinitely better. 

All Might's fight against All For One was great and definitely a really good arc within the season. I didn't even mind the exposition of his students as they watched in horror. The repercussions and fall out of All Might exhausting his power and stepping down as the Symbol of Peace was really great too. I was interested in seeing what would happen with Midoriya because of it this season... but literally nothing happened. BUZZKILL. This is where the season stopped being good. Everything after that went downhill SIGNIFICANTLY. 

I was really disappointed and at most times bored. Like yes, of course as a lover of slice of life, shoujo and characters being happy, I loved the idea of the students living in dorms and seeing what their life is like th

The protagonist is/was someone weak, grows stronger, overtake their heroes/follow their steps(in this case). I wish the story could spread the spotlight to others too(Yes, I know Bakugou has had some too, as much as I love him, he is also one of the mains). The other characters I don't mind but I think I would likeit more if Todoroki was the main protagonist(Okay a little biased but true. If he has something to learn instead of growing stronger then I think I would like him much much more. Of course other characters can be improved too, for example, Bakugo has a softer side instead of yelling all the time, I need him to be calmer. (don't get me wrong he is top 1 fav but it can get annoying.) Todoroki, pretty much no complains but he can't communicate that well with other, not like Midoriya at least. And All might umm idk, he is BIASED but it is obvious why. I don't even care about that anymore. I watched all the way from season one because I am waiting for something good to happen to others and All might(His death would have made Midoriya even strong wouldn't it? No offence) Like Aizawa said, those two(Bakugo&Midoriya) are always in the middle of something big. it is so true, but the spotlight shines brighter on Midoriya. This is a review of the whole BNHA series so far. Lets get something straight here: I really don't enjoy superhero movies or comics. It's really not my thing. Yet. Marathoning this show was probably the most fun and and exasperating task ever. As so many reviews have stated before this one, this show isn't innovative in any way whatsoever. It's a show about superheroes. Another show about evil fighting good. We have all seen that before. It is what it is. How many more shows can be made without them all looking the same? There was an actual TV show called Heroes. HEROES. Let's please berealistic here now. Every single thing that could have been possibly made on superheroes was made. There might be variations, but it's basically all the same.

Boku No Hero Academia is still that. It is still the same thing. Evil fighting good. Good fighting evil. A bunch of heroes trying to get together to fight another group of villains. We have all seen that before. The third season is better. It actually has some cool action scenes. But with this show, I still needed to understand the hype.

The first season was very bland. I tried watching the first season when it first came out and dropped it after only two episodes. The main characters are annoying, both Midoriya and Bakugo. We have all seen these hero tropes before. We have all seen charismatic characters like All Might, and we have all seen secondary characters like Uraraka. So, the characters aren't so compelling. The main character of this show is another 'I have no talents but I have a will of stone'', and the co protagonist is 'I WILL BECOME STRONBER THAN YOU'. Seriously, it felt like I was watching KHR all over again. But- holy shit- these characters make me laugh hard. They make fun of themselves, and they seriously don't care about it.

Still. This isn't new either. So why is it so much fun to watch a bunch of teenagers making fun of themselves and their superpowers/quirks? Maybe because it's one of the most redeeming

Let me start off by saying, even though i gave it a 9, i highly considered granting it the score of 10 simply because of how much i enjoyed watching it. While i've given all three seasons of the show a 9, the third season is in my opinion undoubtedly the best of the three. I won't give out spoilers for those of you who haven't watched it yet, but we got quite a few amazing fight scenes, as well as quite a bit of character developement, which is very important for a show like "my hero academia" which puts a lot of focus on the characters. Whenit comes to the popular anime, they tend to get a lot of hype, which either enhances enjoyment, when the anime actually lives up to those expectations. 
However it can be very hard for a show to actually live up to the high standards set by the audience. But in my opinion the third season of my hero academia completely killed it, and that's about all you have to know.

Overall It's not perfect, but in my opinion it's better than the other seasons and definetly worth watching.

I'm still not entirely sure how it happened, but I was extremely invested in the third season of Boku no Hero Academia. I marathoned the season in approximately two days, and many of the story movements, even those which I knew were coming, caused an emotional response in me. Reading over my reviews of the first two seasons now feels slightly alien: although I can understand the criticisms they don't feel as though they're coming from me. Let's use Deku as an example. Back in Season 1 I called him annoying, on the basis that his character actions were repetitive. Objectively, I still consider him oneof the weaker - certainly less interesting - characters on the show. He doesn't have much in the way of character flaws. His weaknesses are mainly related to the natural limitations of his quirk and the fact that he has not had it for long. Now I think that he's the only appropriate main character this series could have had, and his moments of heroism and self-sacrifice get taken to sufficient extents that they're still entertaining. There are some interesting story turns with him this season too.

As a matter of fact, a lot of the main characters are three-dimensional and develop well. All Might is a truly great character, and the best moment of this season revolves around him - there's no doubt that you know what I mean if you've seen it. Bakugo is great too. His character is full of weaknesses: he's short-tempered, competitive to a fault, overly proud, bad at expressing his emotions, and wary of others. Those flaws make his actions feel more human, and when they're balanced out by his strengths - confidence, decisiveness, skill, and surprising heroism - they give his character a lot of complexity. This season in particular gives him a lot of focus in comparison to the last one, and a lot of those moments are among the highlights.

In contrast, a lot of the minor characters remain one-note. That list does grow shorter with each passing season, but it still represents s

What can I say? Boku No Hero Academia continues to be one of the most enjoyable popular anime out there! I really really enjoyed season three, it continued to be as character-centered and a story packed as season two, and continues to make me feel on the edge of my seat every single episode. Where can I even start? Well, let's start with the story. The storylines of this season are the most nerve-wracking and action-packed of the show up to this season, from the battle in the camp to the emotional and fuckin' serving battle between All For One and All Might. These storylines aredefinitely extremely emotional and make you really care about the characters, and you just want them to succeed! You feel for them and are scared that they might fail and root for them! This all comes down to how great the writing and character development is. Every character continues evolving, they are log away from the cookie-cutter versions of season 1. They are seasoned, thoughtful, and determined characters that deal with the school and the problems they are faced with. For example the small character arc of sparking Yuga Aoyama, in the episode he stops being a side gag to a person who even though they are self-centered, will sacrifice himself for the greater good of his classmates. A character that has doubt and also cares about his classmates. This is what Boku No Hero Academia does, they make simple characters into these fully fletched amazing character. That's what I most loved of this season, the amazing writing. I still feel that Final Fight of All Might...

There are some cons though, such as that I noticed that the animation and art got pretty cheaper from last seasons, which is pretty noticeable for me in the second half. Such as that Todoroki looks lumpy and funky in many scenes in the license test, or that the amazing heavy lined style that the first and second season had, was taken out a lot. Which it saddening for me because I loved the art style so much, but I understand i

the only anime in existence my dad will watch... I don't get it either, I know. He probably just likes seeing the fights. Although the shipping is 2/3 of what I care about... I think it's pretty good. NOT AMAZING, honestly. But I think Deku is a bit edgy, *compared to other shounen protagonists* And they executed a decent story in my opinion, but then again I'm not super harsh. As long as there's a story and the message is heartfelt, idc. I like how every character is followed and grows during the story, instead of just Deku getting the development. You don't have tohate on it, it's good enough is all I'm saying. I don't think I've seen such a shy or generous protagonist *correct me if I'm wrong* that has a fair amount of determination in what he does instead of just being like "Oh I got it, you do this" he actually has a rolemodel, and a kind of person he wants to become which is more admirable of a goal considering he is an anti-hero. But most protagonists are ambitious I suppose. All in all its generic with some twists here and there that make it more inspiring. I think the idea of becoming something you never thought you could  - in a world where no one struggles but you - is a huge theme that makes up for the repetitive story at times. Characters were average, although Bakugou and deku both developed fairly in the whole series. Good, not amazing, but pretty unique in some ways.

Sometimes I like starting out by going through the weaknesses of a show which I why I actually want to mention, Boku no Hero Academia is truly one of my favorite tv-shows. It's got everything a superhero anime needs - cool designs, over the top action beautifully animated by Studio Bones and a protagonist slowly learning how to become the strongest hero. A shounen show with some of the tropes and definitely all of the strengths. It does unfortunately have some of the anime worlds', or should I say manga worlds' weaknesses - such as a pervy character being played off for fun and sometimespretty still frames during dialogue. A character will seem very simple, then infodumps will be made during a battle which works in a manga but feels a bit stilted when you constantly go from adrenaline pumping power fests to down-to-earth chilling scenes of horror-imagery. There's also a questionable choice of sakuga-moment (seemingly perfect flow in animation, which is rare as it will drain the budget) which I felt could have been used in THE big showdown of the season.
Season 3 is also the weakest one so far in terms of story, as it becomes a bit unfocused.

By the miracle of great voice acting, music and last but NOT least, beautiful animation I forgive each and every one of those faults. I enjoyed the frikkin helooza out of this show. It is a mix of the Japanese medium and the American-style superhero stories. Whilst season 2 had the strongest episodes so far, this one has truly developed into an ensemble show where you wanted to see all the characters grow beyond their limits and seeing that new episode available was enough to get my blood pumping.

Plus Ultra!

Leading up to season 3, I thoroughly enjoyed MHA. It was a unique take on high school life and had characters I could relate to as a high school student. However, season 3 to me just seems sort of just there. Season 1 and 2 I was consistently watching these episodes, I wanted to know what would happen next. But after this point, I found something that just destroys that feeling. I could somewhat enjoy season 3. But if I dropped the series I would not care. There were truly amazing parts but, the bad outweighs the good. I will eventually pick up on season 4to see if the series has improved but season 3 has made me wary of watching season 4.

I'd hate to say that this series' staggering number of critics had the last laugh in the end. Well, they won't have it this season, that's for sure. But I had a number of issues with this season compared to the previous ones that have me worried that they will have that last laugh in the forseeable future. First let me just knock out the carry-over attributes of this season right away. The story and characters- provided you actually ARE a fan of BNHA in the first place- are still as excellent as ever. It does nothing new, but does everything well (I hope I'm thefirst one to say that phrase because it's really catchy). The voice acting is also really good and the art and animation is superb when it needs to be. The main issues with this season pertain to the actual execution of this excellent story by the studio Bones. As such the bulk of this review will split down the middle and cover the shows two halves- its cours- individually and with a couple of minor spoilers.

First Cour- 10/10, Perfect: This first half of the show really isn't all that bad. In fact, it's the best this anime series has ever done. There really isn't anything to say about this cour since it was done so darn well.

The thing to note about this cour is that it ends off on one of the fan-favorite arcs of the series and one of the most important in the actual context of the world of the series. Not since One Piece have I ever seen a fictional society actually influenced on such a fundamental level by the actions taken by individual characters. The consequences of what occur here will carry over into the next half of this season, the bulk of the fourth season, and probably stick all the way to the end of the ongoing manga. It's THAT important!

Now if only the second cour was that simple...

Second Cour- 7/10, Hm...: This is where it gets fishy. It is natural to expect that the very next arc after the one in the first cour wouldn't be able to top that previous arc at all. Still, the arc that takes

I started watching MHA long before its fanbase blew up, and by the time Season 1 was over, I was entirely skeptical about how it would turn out as a whole down the road. I didn't want a copy of Naruto, like AT ALL. There are quite a few people who compare MHA to Naruto, and I can understand why since both anime have similar premises under their belts. Fortunately with MHA, it does many things right that Naruto didn't, so much that even the creator of Naruto praises it, and here's why... Ever since the first season, MHA knew what it wanted to be: asuperhero anime. If it's gonna be a superhero anime, then I want to see some superhero-type shit. The problem I've always had with Naruto Shippuden is its own identity crisis with most of it lying under the ridiculous amount of fillers it has. If you're gonna make an action anime about ninjas, then I want you to get to the point and show me some ninja action shit. I don't want to see a 30-minute episode about Naruto trying to remove Kakashi's mask. Hell, MHA even got its own fillers right because the characters weren't engaging in pointless crap that did nothing for them. The fillers provided lessons and hands-on experiences for characters to help them grow, even if the only two fillers in this series so far won't ever be referenced again. 

Still, what makes MHA such a fascinating anime to watch is the fact that there is little to no drag throughout its entirety. It constantly pushes itself to bring entertainment and intensity without making you wait for something to happen. It simply gets straight to the point and it keeps you interested. MHA is intense, funny, epic, and just purely entertaining.

Season 3 brought back pretty much everything we should already be familiar with in this anime, yet, with every episode, it strives to go bigger. It constantly adds more and more hype to each scene, but again, the anime doesn't make you wait 10 episodes for a teased battle. It gets straight into the shit. Season 3 had a monumenta

Pure, unleaded shounen awesomeness. My Hero Academia. Whether you have watched the series or not, you have at least heard the name. A lot. It, along with Attack on Titan, have undoubtedly been the hottest things in the anime world over the last several years. And with popularity comes opinions. There are plenty of fans that will decry this series as overrated or at least overhyped. To be honest, I was sort of in that camp before this season. I liked, but did not love, season 1 or 2. It was a fun series but nothing about it popped off the screen to me. I wouldwatch an episode and usually not think about it until the next week. 

This changed in season 3. The fights reached new levels of hype. Multiple characters interesting or fun enough to establish themselves as favorites throughout anime community. The music and voice acting were incredible across the board. The big moments felt... well, big. And more visceral. Everything about the third season was a step ahead of the first two.

In fact, while some may consider this blasphemous, season 3 approached Hunter x Hunter (2011)-levels of battle shounen fun and euphoria for me. If an anime can give me goosebumps, it's doing something right, and this season of MHA was one of the few do accomplish this feat. It quickly became one of those shows I looked forward to each and every week. 

Really the only thing keeping me from scoring this a 10 was the 2nd half couldn't replicate the excellence of the 1st. Other that, it gave me about everything I could ask for from this genre.

To understand my thoughts better, you might want to check my reviews of the previous seasons. This way you get context for some of the things I say. I'm also going to copy what mother's basement said in his video. He made very good points, so I'll feature them. The next season of my hero academia finished, and this show just keeps on getting better. I started watching my hero academia when the second season finished. I found the first season to be good, but it was really the second season that got me to be interested in what the show was going to go. So nowthat the third season has finished, I can finally say that one of the more lacking characters in the show has been saved.

Bakugo, from the start isn't presented as a good character. He bullies Izuku and drags down his dreams. But to me, felt like a very good character in the first season. You could see how terrified he was that he wasn't as good as he might have thought. 
But in the second season I felt that he didn't develop as much as in the first one. I didn't get why he seemed so mad all the time. But I think I figured it out now.

It's one episode the comes to mind when I think of bakugo in the third season. In the of the later episodes, how do I say this while not spoiling anything, you get to another perspective of bakugo. We learn why Bakugo might have done the things he did. And if you go back in earlier episodes, you can see it clearly. We see how they develop bakugos insecurities that I saw in the first season. How his powerful quirk is making him feel like he should be more than he is. He feels responsible for things he shouldn't. 
Bakugo shows a lot of humanity in this season. 
This is probably my favorite part of this season, or the show as a whole.

But I had some problems though. Most of the arcs felt very forgettable in this season. 
My favorite part about the second was how much it packed into so few arcs. It had a tournament arc and then a stain arc (and a small arc at the end). I found it very

The first cour was amazing. The fights and the story were top tier. Bones did a great job in making emotional scenes. The buildup and context made the fights 100% better. The second cour fell. Most of the story was boring, with 1 episode of filler. The scenes just didn't live up to the 1st cour. Some characters were just plain annoying. The characters made this story feel amazing. The fights were brilliant because of the characters. Without the character's backround, the fights would just be boring. Bones did an excellent job with animation this season. The fights were full of fluid movement. You can easily tell Bonesgave extra effort for this show. The sound was amazing, especially at crucial moments. The sound alone can change your emotions while watching.

I want to keep this review as precise and quick as possible. Story 9/10: It's a cliche plot, a kid with no power inherit power from the strongest hero and becomes the number 1 himself. But Horikoshi's able to turn such a simple concept into something so different, so entertaining, so unique. And it's all thanks to its great characters. Art 10/10: This is on a personal preference. I personally like the art and I think it's great. Also the characters all have memorable designs. They're the type that's hard to forget once you see their appearances/costumes. Also, the girls are cute. Sound 10/10: The OSTs this seasonmannnn!!! Lit AF!!! Also the ED "Long Happy Philia" is my fav one so far from bnha.

Character 9/10: Bnha has a vast variety of distinctive, memorable, and unique characters, and they're the main reason I like this series so much. I don't even need a plot, just two great characters interacting with each others is enough. Makes you wonder what would happen if Uraraka interacts with Shigaraki, or Aoyama interacts with Dabi, or Shinsou and Monoma...etc. Just put two characters in a room and a plot will generate by itself. I didn't give it a 10/10 because of Mineta, the little grape fuck. Horikoshi added him for comedy but I think that's unnecessary. Aoyama and Monoma are enough comedy. We don't need Mineta. Plus, his actions are creepy and borderline sexual assault. Please, get him out of the picture.

Enjoyment 10/10: I've never liked a series so much before. This season is better than season 1 and 2, enjoyment wise. I've forgotten how many times I've cried while watching it (let me count, OFA vs AFO, Izuku vs Muscular......). The last anime that drove me to tears was Anohana, and that's a romance anime. For me to cry over a shounen anime is like Bakugo not cursing for a day. Impossible. But bnha is able to do it. That just shows how invested I'm in.

Overall 10/10: Cannot wait for the next season. In the mean time, Imma go read some fanfictions now...

Maybe this is about the point where people started to make contrarian comments about how MHA isn't that good given how they seem to be some of the most popular reviews on here for this season. I don't really understand what they're going for as their only really complaint seems to be that they've seen stuff like this before, which is basically the point of a genre, subgenre, and in part what makes things popular. It doesn't feel like an honest criticism when it's so dependent on other circumstances around it rather than an honest reaction and engagement with the art. But anyways, that's mypiece on that.

My Hero Academia - Season 3 (Review): I honestly don't have all too much to say about this season compared to the previous two. It's still good, for sure. But if I'm being honest, it takes a pretty big decline after the first couple arcs. This season features another 3 arcs to go through so I guess I'll just talk about them individually again. Forest Training Camp Arc: Out of the three, this one is probably second in the tier list from season 3. It takes a little bit of a step into being a darker show once again. I liked the fight with muscular, it was somedecent development for Midoriya and it was a fairly entertaining fight by itself. But if I'm honest, other than being fully introduced to the league of villains and Bakugo and on of the Pussycats being kidnapped, nothing else happened this arc. Everyone, once again, survives the encounter and I'm kind of thinking that My Hero Academia doesn't seem to have the balls to do something big yet I don't know when they're going to begin taking things seriously (hopefully in season 5) but I really wish that everyone would stop coming out of these dangerous encounters unscathed pretty much. The elite squad of villains was fun and I liked seeing what they could do and I also liked how they had different beliefs, one of them refusing to kill Deku because Stain acknowledged him as a real hero - I thought that was great. But other than those things that I mentioned, not much else happened here so this arc is like a 7.5-8/10 maybe, not as good as the Tournament or Hero Killer Arcs from last season sadly.

Hideout Raid Arc:
Here we go, here's the good shit. Whilst I think that this season is overall not as great as the last one, I genuinely believe that this arc is the peak of My Hero Academia so far. The introduction to All For One is one of the best entrances for an anime villain and this guy is honestly the reason why I think the music for this season is the peak for this show. All For One's main theme is bone chilling and fro

Story 8/10 The story of season three involves three major events firstly we see the students of class 1A attacked by the League of Villains while on a training camp which requires Deku and classmates to go all out to defend themselves and others. We then see All Might in his last battle against his most dangerous foe. The final half of season three sees Deku and his classmates take on the exam for their provisional hero license which requires them to firstly work as a team and secondly they have to be calm under the pressure of being in a tough fluid situation. The first halfthe season is probably the best of My Hero Academia but the second half was also of a pretty high standard.

Art 8/10

This is best when there is plenty of action and there was quite a lot of action in season three overall very good animation here.

Sound 7/10

Wasn't a big fan of the music this season although I felt the second half was better than the first. Sound effects and voice acting remained good throughout.

Characters 9/10

The students of 1A are clearly the stars but we met some other good characters this season from UA, other schools and the villains. Overall a really good season in this category.

Enjoyment 9/10

I continue to really enjoy the show with plenty of action to enjoy and big set piece moments I had a lot of fun this season.

Overall 8/10

It was a little while after the season had finished airing, in fact I think I was watching season 4 at the time I had this thought, but I came to a realization of something: "I think I've been taking Boku no Hero for granted." I don't think I was fully appreciating anymore exactly how good this show was. It's sometimes hard to see past the hype and the mainstream attention of a series like this to the core of what actually makes it good. It's easy to quickly write something off as "oh it's another tournament arc" or "oh look at that they're ripping offNaruto again." When you strip away the comparisons to other similar works, when you ignore the requisite dozens of genderbent body-positive Todoroki cosplayers at literally every convention since 2016 and the sheer ubiquity of lewd versions of that yandere girl who likes the blood, what you have is competent storytelling and worldbuilding being executing in a spectacular way.

All this to say, I usually hate stuff that's popular because once something hits the mainstream it no longer feels special. When people start bandwagoning onto a show from outside of the culture and fandom typically associated with anime, they usually want to bring everything that isn't anime into it with them. Western creators often bend the knee to appease these new "fans" and end up destroying everything that made their works unique. The western comic books My Hero takes it's inspiration from are now rife with diversity and gender identity politics, making it less about the story or characters and more about propaganda for a particular political ideal. It's disgusting.

Thankfully the outrage mob that is western social media still has little sway over the influence of Japanese creators. Despite facing plenty of outcries of "scandals," the fun police has largely been ignored, which I find delightful. It doesn't really reflect much on the quality of the story or this anime adaptation, I'm just talking about the fandom surrounding this show in ge

'My Hero Academia' is a popular, long-running action show about teenage superheroes punching each other. I watched three seasons, and in the end, I got nothing out of it. The characters seem exceptionally determined, but they lack any recognizable motivation, so it doesn't matter how often or how loudly they fly around shouting catchphrases about saving the world or protecting their friends' smiles: when the novelty wears off, none of it is relatable. There are also far too many of them. Essentially, they all compete to see whose superpower makes them punch the hardest so they can pass their exams and get a civil service job,which is not very heroic. Every arc is a tournament arc, but the tournaments don't really matter. Who cares about your high score? You're punching your classmates.

I wanted to enjoy the show, I followed the story closely, and although I noticed early on the slow pace of the plot, I stuck with it until season three — I think it was the fifth or sixth tournament arc. At that point, I realized that the show isn't going anywhere. Of course, punches will continue to be thrown, buildings will continue to collapse, plot-related events will continue to trickle past, etc., but the show simply does not have anything to say and will never reward my invested time. At least let the main character date a girl!

The fight scenes were well animated, but I don't really care about the technical details. The voice acting is pretty good, the music is fine, and the English subtitles are acceptable.

Truthfully, I can't explain the popularity of a largely pointless action-style entertainment product. If you want to see teenagers fight with magic powers, and you also want a show with a heart and a brain, you might enjoy 'Chivalry of a Failed Knight' or 'The Misfit of Demon King Academy', and maybe someday one of them will get a second season.

If there is any word to describe MHA series in general, it's polished. There is nothing too new about MHA's story or characters, but it's done so well that those issues become a minor niggle. There are a good number of fights this season that was amazing, and the general cast development is praiseworthy as well. If MHA keepsat its pace, it might be the 2nd long-running shounen to earn the distinctive ten from me. The third season of the My Hero Academia anime series was produced by Bones and directed by Kenji Nagasaki. Like the rest of the series, it adapts Kōhei Horikoshi's My Hero Academia manga from the rest of the eighth volume through the beginning of the 14th volume over 25 episodes and follows the adventures of Izuku Midoriya. The third season ran from April 7[1][2] to September 29, 2018 on ytv in Japan. Toho released the season on DVD and Blu-Ray in eight compilations, each containing two to four episodes, between July 18, 2018, and February 13, 2019. Funimation licensed the season for an English-language release inNorth America and released the first compilation on May 7, 2019. Their adaptation premiered on Adult Swim's Toonami block on March 3, 2019.[3]

Four pieces of theme music are used for this season: two opening themes and two ending themes. For the first thirteen episodes, the opening theme is "Odd Future" by Uverworld and the first ending theme is "Update" (アップデート) by miwa.[4] For the rest of the season, the second opening theme is "Make my Story" by Lenny Code Fiction and the ending theme is "Long Hope Philia" (ロングホープ・フィリア) by Masaki Suda.[5]

My Hero Academia season 3 is where things finally get serious. The first time I watched this anime, I didn't remember how much the civil pressure affecting the heroes and U.A. was present. I like that approach, and it gives the anime more seriousness. They are no longer just heroes fighting regardless of the consequences; now they are heroes trying to face the consequences of the damage they cause and it becomes noticeable in the incredible fight that Might has against AFO and in other episodes. I think this is a noteworthy point of this anime, since in others of this type, the epic takes precedenceover the coherent and logical.

The downside is the strange character relationships that emerge throughout the episodes, the slow progression, and the constant misplaced jokes.

So far this is my favorite season and I hope the next one is just as good.

When coming from the first two seasons of this anime, you end up noticing the patterns in it that carry through each season. The third season is no exception. There isn't much to say about the story this time around. It's a clear continuation to the past seasons while trying to add new aspects and polish old ones at the same time. The story isn't anything special when it comes to originality, and this season shows this the strongest. This season seems to be focused on setting up the groundwork for future events and interactions and is less focused on progressing as much as possible. Thistime around, the story seems more dark and awkward, while also trying to transition to whatever it has in store. Not a bad story, but when compared to S2, this is a downgrade.

The characters are as I've stated in my reviews for the past two seasons, so I won't be repeating it here. If you want to know what I fully said, then go and read my reviews on the past two seasons of this anime. However, in addition to what I've already said before, the characters, do get toned down a bit, as if being set up for something in the future. This anime really likes to set things up for later when the time comes.

The animation is of the same quality as the past two seasons, with its quality leaning more towards the quality of season two, and maybe more. There are no dips in quality whatsoever when just watching, and sometimes the quality increases instead. Overall, good animation and art, as always.

Overall, a good third season for this anime. This series has promised to become something great, but as of right now, it is great so far. This season is a bit lackluster when compared to the second season, but is still good. If you've enjoyed the other seasons so far, then you'll like this season as well.
